cves.description:
evernote-mcp-server openBrowser Command Injection Privilege Escalation Vulnerability. This vulnerability allows local attackers to escalate privileges on affected installations of evernote-mcp-server. An attacker must first obtain the ability to execute low-privileged code on the target system in order to exploit this vulnerability.

The specific flaw exists within the openBrowser function. The issue results from the lack of proper validation of a user-supplied string before using it to execute
cves.how_introduced: The blamed commit introduced the `openBrowser` function using `exec` to execute shell commands with user-supplied URLs, allowing arbitrary command execution via crafted URLs.
cves.root_cause: The openBrowser function used the shell-executing exec() function with a template literal to open URLs, allowing an attacker to inject arbitrary shell commands via a crafted URL string.

bug_commits.author: brentmid
bug_commits.message: Add comprehensive OAuth 1.0a authentication and testing suite

llm_verdict.reasoning: The blamed commit is the initial creation of the `auth.js` file, which introduced the `openBrowser` function. This function used `exec` with a template literal to open URLs, directly leading to the command injection vulnerability.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: The openBrowser function used the shell-executing exec() function with a template literal to open URLs, allowing an attacker to inject arbitrary shell commands via a crafted URL string.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: exec(`${command} "${url}"`, (error) => { ... })
llm_verdict.causal_chain: The blamed commit introduced the `openBrowser` function using `exec` to execute shell commands with user-supplied URLs, allowing arbitrary command execution via crafted URLs.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The fix specifically removes `child_process.exec()` from `auth.js`'s `openBrowser()` and replaces it with `spawn()` plus argument arrays to avoid shell interpretation. Blame on the pre-fix file shows the security-relevant lines — including `const { exec } = require('child_process')`, `function openBrowser(url)`, and `exec(`${command} "${url}"`, ...)` — were introduced by commit `e08547bcdb42aaa86190c6e2dfc64159fcd3a146`. `auth.js` did not exist in the parent commit (`9f7c1b36d698845ea8bd968ad7446550995a2a3d`), and later commits only made incidental changes around logging/token storage, not the unsafe exec pattern, so the suspected commit authored the vulnerable code path.
evidence: Fix commit `1e66c78c4ce6ea294ac6b0eb289a9eae9c5e9579` rewrites `openBrowser()` from `exec(`${command} "${url}"`)` to `spawn(command, args, ...)`, explicitly addressing command injection in `auth.js`.
Pre-fix file state (`auth.js` at `b8444aa20c32eefdb3bf38ab02f81904a80c4533`) still contains the vulnerable code: `exec(`${command} "${url}"`, (error) => { ... })` in `openBrowser()`.

`blame_lines` on that pre-fix state attributes line 347 (`exec(...)`) and the surrounding `openBrowser` function definition lines 343-356 to `e08547bcdb42aaa86190c6e2dfc64159fcd3a146`; line 10 (`const { exec } = require('child_process');`) is also blamed to `e08547bc`.

`git_log` for `auth.js` shows the file history starts at `e08547bc...`, and `get_file_at_commit` for parent commit `9f7c1b36d698845ea8bd968ad7446550995a2a3d` fails because `auth.js` did not exist there, confirming the vulnerable function was created in the suspected commit.

Subsequent commits touching `auth.js` (e.g. `613ce9c4e952edc98b57e7d9be53df70bf07e65d`) only changed logging calls in `openBrowser` (`console.log` → `console.error`) and did not introduce the unsafe shell execution.

bug_commits.author: brentmid
bug_commits.message: Fix JSON-RPC logging conflicts and update documentation

llm_verdict.reasoning: The blamed commit only performs refactoring of console logging statements (changing console.log to console.error) within the existing openBrowser function. The vulnerable pattern using exec() was already present in the code prior to this commit, meaning this commit did not introduce the vulnerability.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: The openBrowser function used the shell-executing exec() function with a template literal to open URLs, allowing an attacker to inject arbitrary shell commands via a crafted URL string.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: exec(`${command} "${url}"`, (error) => { ... })

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The vulnerable pattern fixed in 1e66c78 is the use of `child_process.exec()` with a shell-interpolated URL in `openBrowser()`. Blame on the pre-fix file (`b8444aa`) shows the security-relevant lines—`const { exec } = require('child_process')` and `exec(`${command} "${url}"`, ...)`—were introduced by commit `e08547bc`, not by the suspected commit `613ce9c4`. Commit `613ce9c4` only changed surrounding logging from stdout/warn to stderr/error inside the already-existing function, so it is a trivial blame-overlap change rather than the bug-introducing change.
evidence: `blame_lines` on `auth.js` at pre-fix commit `b8444aa20c32eefdb3bf38ab02f81904a80c4533` attributes `function openBrowser(url)`, the platform command selection, and the vulnerable sink `exec(`${command} "${url}"`, ...)` (lines 343-347) to `e08547bcdb42aaa86190c6e2dfc64159fcd3a146`; only non-security log lines 350-351 and 353 are attributed to `613ce9c4`.

`get_file_at_commit` for the suspected BIC's parent `4dd2d8c90a1202762e20bf3237f91d064711f06a` already contains the same vulnerable code: `exec(`${command} "${url}"`, ...)` in `openBrowser()`, proving the command-injection pattern existed before `613ce9c4`.

`get_commit_diff` for `613ce9c4e952edc98b57e7d9be53df70bf07e65d` shows only logging changes in `openBrowser()` (`console.log`/`console.warn` -> `console.error`) and no change to the `exec()` call or URL interpolation. By contrast, `get_commit_diff` for `e08547bcdb42aaa86190c6e2dfc64159fcd3a146` shows `auth.js` was added with `const { exec } = require('child_process');` and the original vulnerable `openBrowser()` implementation.

cves.description: A vulnerability has been found in mruby up to 3.4.0. This vulnerability affects the function sort_cmp of the file src/array.c. Such manipulation leads to use after free. An attack has to be approached locally. The exploit has been disclosed to the public and may be used. The name of the patch is eb398971bfb43c38db3e04528b68ac9a7ce509bc. It is advisable to implement a patch to correct this issue.
cves.how_introduced: 1. The blamed commit introduced a new version of `sort_cmp` that performs callbacks to user-defined Ruby code. 2. During these callbacks, the array can be modified (e.g., elements popped), changing its length without necessarily triggering a pointer reallocation. 3. The blamed commit only checked if the pointer `p` changed, allowing the sort to continue on an array with a modified length. 4. This leads to out-of-bounds access or use-after-free when the sorting algorithm continues to use the stale length or pointer.
cves.root_cause: The sort function failed to detect modifications to the array's length during comparison callbacks, leading to potential memory corruption when the array was reallocated while being accessed.

bug_commits.author: Yukihiro "Matz" Matsumoto
bug_commits.message: array: implement hybrid sorting with algorithm selection

llm_verdict.reasoning: The blamed commit refactored the `sort_cmp` function to use values instead of indices but failed to properly validate the array's state after the comparison callback. Specifically, it removed the check `size < a || size < b` and replaced it with only a pointer check `RARRAY_PTR(ary) != p`, neglecting to check if the array length (`RARRAY_LEN`) had changed, which is the exact check added in the fix.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: The sort function failed to detect modifications to the array's length during comparison callbacks, leading to potential memory corruption when the array was reallocated while being accessed.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: if (RARRAY_PTR(ary) != p) { mrb_raise(...); }
llm_verdict.causal_chain: 1. The blamed commit introduced a new version of `sort_cmp` that performs callbacks to user-defined Ruby code. 2. During these callbacks, the array can be modified (e.g., elements popped), changing its length without necessarily triggering a pointer reallocation. 3. The blamed commit only checked if the pointer `p` changed, allowing the sort to continue on an array with a modified length. 4. This leads to out-of-bounds access or use-after-free when the sorting algorithm continues to use the stale length or pointer.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The fix addresses a missing array-length consistency check in `sort_cmp`: before the fix, the code only verified that `RARRAY_PTR(ary)` was unchanged, which misses in-place realloc/shrink cases where the pointer stays the same but the length changes. Blame on the pre-fix file (`00e7474ccefb...`) attributes the key vulnerable lines—including the pointer-only check in `sort_cmp` and the updated caller sites in `heapify`/`insertion_sort`—to `cf8faed585e14ab57cc390173d1b571aee438390`. Although an earlier version already had imperfect modification checks, this commit rewrote `sort_cmp` to pass values instead of indices and removed the prior length/index-based validation, thereby authoring the exact vulnerable code that the fix patches.
evidence: In the pre-fix state (`00e7474ccefb0104a2c0bef738e0392932902172`), `blame_lines` shows line 2094 `if (RARRAY_PTR(ary) != p) {` was last changed by `cf8faed585e14ab57cc390173d1b571aee438390`; the fix changes this to `if (RARRAY_PTR(ary) != p || RARRAY_LEN(ary) != n)` and moves snapshotting of `p`/`n` into `sort_cmp`.

`get_file_at_commit` for the BIC parent (`6fab19cd8221f978705aaad98112dbd9a385d859`) shows the previous implementation had `mrb_int size = RARRAY_LEN(ary); if (RARRAY_PTR(ary) != p || size < a || size < b)`, so `cf8faed...` removed the length/index-based part when it rewrote `sort_cmp` to take `a_val`/`b_val` instead of indices.

The BIC diff itself shows it changed `sort_cmp` signature and callers from index-based comparisons to value-based ones, and introduced `insertion_sort`; the later fix patches those exact BIC-authored call sites (`heapify` and `insertion_sort`) to stop passing `p` and to recheck both pointer and length after each comparison.

cves.description: Claude Code permission deny bypass through symlink
cves.how_introduced: 1. The blamed commit introduced the `RuleEngine` class to evaluate security rules. 2. It implemented logic to extract 'file_path' from tool inputs and compare them against deny patterns. 3. It failed to include any path normalization or symlink resolution (like os.path.realpath) during this extraction, allowing users to bypass rules by providing symlinks to restricted files.
cves.root_cause: The application checked file paths against deny rules without resolving symlinks first, allowing attackers to bypass restrictions by creating symlinks to restricted files.

bug_commits.author: Daisy S. Hollman
bug_commits.message: feat: Add hookify plugin for custom hook rules via markdown

llm_verdict.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced the `RuleEngine` class and the logic for evaluating deny rules against tool inputs. Specifically, it implemented the `_rule_matches` and field extraction logic that directly used raw `file_path` strings from `tool_input` without any symlink resolution, creating the vulnerability described in CVE-2025-59829.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: The application checked file paths against deny rules without resolving symlinks first, allowing attackers to bypass restrictions by creating symlinks to restricted files.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: Checking file paths against deny rules without using os.path.realpath() to resolve symlinks.
llm_verdict.causal_chain: 1. The blamed commit introduced the `RuleEngine` class to evaluate security rules. 2. It implemented logic to extract 'file_path' from tool inputs and compare them against deny patterns. 3. It failed to include any path normalization or symlink resolution (like os.path.realpath) during this extraction, allowing users to bypass rules by providing symlinks to restricted files.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The fix addresses a specific flaw in `plugins/hookify/core/rule_engine.py`: `_extract_field()` compared deny-rule `file_path` conditions against the raw user-supplied path instead of a canonicalized real path, allowing symlink targets to evade matching. In the pre-fix state, the vulnerable returns (`return value` for direct `tool_input['file_path']`, plus raw `tool_input.get('file_path', '')` in file-tool branches) all blame to 59372c09, and that same commit created `rule_engine.py` as a brand-new file. `git log` for this file shows only the creation commit and the security fix, so this was not a refactor or blame drift case; 59372c09 authored the vulnerable code that the fix later patched.
evidence: `blame_lines` on pre-fix `plugins/hookify/core/rule_engine.py` shows lines 196-199 (`if field in tool_input` ... `return value`) and lines 243-248 (raw `file_path` returns for file tools) were introduced by commit 59372c0921b0170e81f9c63777962d02347411d5.

`get_commit_diff` for fix commit ff0fdc06760d9d8d94cf45549908e06e2ffe36d6 shows the security change was to add `_resolve_symlink_path()` and use it when extracting `file_path` values, directly patching the raw-path logic in `rule_engine.py`.

`get_commit_diff` for 59372c0921b0170e81f9c63777962d02347411d5 shows `plugins/hookify/core/rule_engine.py` was added as a new file (`new file mode 100644`, `/dev/null -> 8244c00`), meaning this commit wrote the vulnerable implementation from scratch.

`git_log` for `plugins/hookify/core/rule_engine.py` shows only two commits on the file: the creation commit 59372c09 and the security fix ff0fdc06, with no earlier history indicating prior code or a move/rename.

bug_commits.message: feat: Add hookify plugin for custom hook rules via markdown

evidence: `blame_lines` on pre-fix `plugins/hookify/core/rule_engine.py` shows lines 196-199 (`if field in tool_input` ... `return value`) and lines 243-248 (raw `file_path` returns for file tools) were introduced by commit 59372c0921b0170e81f9c63777962d02347411d5.

`get_commit_diff` for fix commit ff0fdc06760d9d8d94cf45549908e06e2ffe36d6 shows the security change was to add `_resolve_symlink_path()` and use it when extracting `file_path` values, directly patching the raw-path logic in `rule_engine.py`.

`get_commit_diff` for 59372c0921b0170e81f9c63777962d02347411d5 shows `plugins/hookify/core/rule_engine.py` was added as a new file (`new file mode 100644`, `/dev/null -> 8244c00`), meaning this commit wrote the vulnerable implementation from scratch.

`git_log` for `plugins/hookify/core/rule_engine.py` shows only two commits on the file: the creation commit 59372c09 and the security fix ff0fdc06, with no earlier history indicating prior code or a move/rename.

cves.description: A flaw has been found in mruby up to 3.4.0. This affects the function mrb_vm_exec of the file src/vm.c of the component JMPNOT-to-JMPIF Optimization. Executing a manipulation can lead to use after free. The attack needs to be launched locally. The exploit has been published and may be used. This patch is called e50f15c1c6e131fa7934355eb02b8173b13df415. It is advisable to implement a patch to correct this issue.
cves.how_introduced: 1. Blamed commit introduces an optimization that assumes the instruction at `fail_pos - 2` is always `OP_JMPNOT`. 2. The optimization unconditionally overwrites this byte with `OP_JMPIF` if certain PC-relative conditions are met. 3. In cases involving pinned variables, the instruction is actually `OP_JMP`, leading to an invalid instruction sequence and subsequent memory corruption/UAF in the VM.
cves.root_cause: The compiler incorrectly optimized a JMP instruction into a JMPIF instruction without verifying the original opcode, leading to an invalid instruction sequence that causes memory corruption during VM execution.

bug_commits.author: Yukihiro "Matz" Matsumoto
bug_commits.message: mruby-compiler: optimize JMPNOT+JMP to JMPIF in pattern matching

llm_verdict.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced the 'Optimize: single JMPNOT can be inverted to JMPIF' logic which directly modifies the instruction stream at `s->iseq[fail_pos - 2]`. It failed to verify that the instruction at that offset was actually an `OP_JMPNOT`, leading to incorrect opcode replacement when other instructions (like `OP_JMP` from pinned variables) occupied that position.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: The compiler incorrectly optimized a JMP instruction into a JMPIF instruction without verifying the original opcode, leading to an invalid instruction sequence that causes memory corruption during VM execution.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: s->iseq[fail_pos - 2] = OP_JMPIF; // performed without verifying that s->iseq[fail_pos - 2] was originally OP_JMPNOT
llm_verdict.causal_chain: 1. Blamed commit introduces an optimization that assumes the instruction at `fail_pos - 2` is always `OP_JMPNOT`. 2. The optimization unconditionally overwrites this byte with `OP_JMPIF` if certain PC-relative conditions are met. 3. In cases involving pinned variables, the instruction is actually `OP_JMP`, leading to an invalid instruction sequence and subsequent memory corruption/UAF in the VM.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The fix commit adds a missing opcode check (`s->iseq[fail_pos - 2] == OP_JMPNOT`) before rewriting bytecode, because the optimization incorrectly assumed every `fail_pos` came from a 4-byte `JMPNOT`; for undefined pinned variables, `NODE_PAT_PIN` can supply a 3-byte `OP_JMP` instead, causing bytecode corruption. Blame on the pre-fix file attributes the vulnerable optimization block, including the unsafe `s->iseq[fail_pos - 2] = OP_JMPIF`, directly to 2b72d8a7c153e2afb22245ad9e40e0c7d5b1aa70. The parent commit 3af15e0012e6a4955f46f5f40f6d5b9f8d6e76aa does not contain this optimization at all, while the conflicting `NODE_PAT_PIN` `OP_JMP` path already existed earlier in 1de6340f1bc81564274890660f66444e48d660b0, so 2b72d8 introduced the faulty assumption and the exact code the fix patches.
evidence: Fix commit e50f15c1 message: the JMPNOT-to-JMPIF optimization assumed `fail_pos` always came from `JMPNOT`; undefined pinned vars in `NODE_PAT_PIN` can instead generate `OP_JMP`, so `fail_pos - 2` corrupts bytecode. The patch adds `s->iseq[fail_pos - 2] == OP_JMPNOT`.

Blame at pre-fix commit c46f9e079364fdc959abfbb379651d6fc5867044 for codegen.c lines 6628-6635 attributes the optimization block and `s->iseq[fail_pos - 2] = OP_JMPIF;` to 2b72d8a7c153e2afb22245ad9e40e0c7d5b1aa70.

At BIC parent 3af15e0012e6a4955f46f5f40f6d5b9f8d6e76aa, the code simply did `match_pos = genjmp(s, OP_JMP, JMPLINK_START);` followed by `dispatch_linked(s, fail_pos);`; searches show no `fail_pos - 2` / JMPNOT-to-JMPIF optimization existed before 2b72d8.

Earlier commit 1de6340f1bc81564274890660f66444e48d660b0 already added `NODE_PAT_PIN` logic where undefined pinned variables use `tmp = genjmp(s, OP_JMP, *fail_pos);`, proving the unsafe assumption introduced by 2b72d8 was invalid from the moment it landed.

cves.description:
A flaw was found in mirror-registry where an authenticated user can trick the system into accessing unintended internal or restricted systems by providing malicious web addresses. 

When the application processes these addresses, it automatically follows redirects without verifying the final destination, allowing attackers to route requests to systems they should not have access to.
cves.how_introduced: 1. Blamed commit introduces HarborAdapter and the logic to query external registry APIs. 2. The implementation uses a request handler that does not check for or block HTTP redirects. 3. This allows an attacker-controlled registry to return a 3xx redirect to an internal/loopback IP, leading to SSRF.
cves.root_cause: The application processes user-supplied URLs and follows redirects without validating if the resolved IP addresses point to internal or restricted networks, allowing attackers to access internal systems.

bug_commits.author: Shaon H
bug_commits.message: feat(mirror): add repository discovery endpoint with registry adapters (PROJQUAY-1266)

llm_verdict.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced the `util/orgmirror/harbor_adapter.py` file and the logic for interacting with external registries. The original implementation failed to explicitly handle HTTP redirect status codes (3xx), allowing the underlying HTTP client to follow redirects to potentially restricted internal IP addresses, which is the root cause of the SSRF vulnerability.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: The application processes user-supplied URLs and follows redirects without validating if the resolved IP addresses point to internal or restricted networks, allowing attackers to access internal systems.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: Fetching user-supplied URLs and following redirects without validating the final resolved IP against a blocklist of private/internal networks.
llm_verdict.causal_chain: 1. Blamed commit introduces HarborAdapter and the logic to query external registry APIs. 2. The implementation uses a request handler that does not check for or block HTTP redirects. 3. This allows an attacker-controlled registry to return a 3xx redirect to an internal/loopback IP, leading to SSRF.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The fix adds an explicit 3xx check in `HarborAdapter.list_repositories()` because the adapter’s request/response logic did not safely reject redirects. Blame on the pre-fix file (`9afe28a5`, the state before `5ad2b732`) shows the core vulnerable block—the `self.session.get(...)` call and the surrounding status handling/`raise_for_status()` logic—was originally written in `3869d001`; only `allow_redirects=False` was added later by the intermediate security fix. The suspected BIC also created `util/orgmirror/harbor_adapter.py` as a new file, so this was not a move/refactor of older code but the introduction of the Harbor adapter and its redirect-following behavior.
evidence: `5ad2b732` patches `util/orgmirror/harbor_adapter.py` by adding `elif 300 <= response.status_code < 400: raise HarborDiscoveryException(...)` inside `HarborAdapter.list_repositories()`.

Blame on `9afe28a53b85fc40ce367cc78a5addcead88abe1` for lines 74-99 shows `3869d001` authored the `self.session.get(...)` call and the existing HTTP status handling / `response.raise_for_status()` block; only line 80 (`allow_redirects=False`) came from `9afe28a5`.

`9afe28a5`'s diff shows the SSRF fix added `allow_redirects=False` to HarborAdapter requests, confirming the original code in `3869d001` had left Requests’ default redirect-following behavior in place.

`3869d001`'s diff creates `util/orgmirror/harbor_adapter.py` as a new file (`new file mode 100644`), introducing the Harbor adapter and its vulnerable request path rather than merely moving existing code.

cves.description: eBay API MCP Server Affected by Environment Variable Injection
cves.how_introduced: The blamed commit added a custom function to update the `.env` file using string manipulation and regular expressions instead of a secure parser. By dynamically constructing a regex with the `key` variable without sanitization, it created an injection vulnerability that the fix commit later resolved by using the `dotenv` and `dotenv-stringify` libraries.
cves.root_cause: The application used insecure regular expressions to update the .env file, allowing an attacker to inject arbitrary environment variables or manipulate file structure by providing crafted input keys or values.

bug_commits.author: Claude
bug_commits.message: feat: Auto-update .env file with refreshed tokens

llm_verdict.reasoning: The blamed commit introduces the `updateEnvFile` function, which contains the exact insecure regular expression (`new RegExp('^(#\\s*)?${key}=.*$', 'gm')`) identified as the root cause of the vulnerability. This regex allows attackers to inject arbitrary environment variables.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: The application used insecure regular expressions to update the .env file, allowing an attacker to inject arbitrary environment variables or manipulate file structure by providing crafted input keys or values.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: new RegExp(`^(#\\s*)?${key}=.*$`, 'gm')
llm_verdict.causal_chain: The blamed commit added a custom function to update the `.env` file using string manipulation and regular expressions instead of a secure parser. By dynamically constructing a regex with the `key` variable without sanitization, it created an injection vulnerability that the fix commit later resolved by using the `dotenv` and `dotenv-stringify` libraries.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The fix hardens `src/auth/oauth.ts` by replacing the hand-rolled `.env` editing logic in `updateEnvFile()` with `dotenv.parse()` + `dotenv-stringify()`, eliminating direct string interpolation of token values into `.env` content. In the last vulnerable revision (`8c1989e3`), git blame shows that the helper body, the `writeFileSync(envPath, envContent, 'utf-8')`, and the new call sites that persist tokens to `.env` were introduced by `4c9c826c`; later commits only made minor follow-up changes such as adding `existsSync`, changing the type syntax, and quoting values to fix `#` truncation. The parent commit `a00b6c59` had no `updateEnvFile()` at all and did not automatically persist tokens to `.env`, so `4c9c826c` is the commit that introduced the vulnerable behavior.
evidence: `aab0bda7` replaces manual `.env` line construction/replacement in `updateEnvFile()` with `dotenv.parse()` and `dotenv-stringify()`, showing the vulnerable pattern was ad-hoc text generation from token values.

Blame on pre-fix `src/auth/oauth.ts` (`8c1989e3`) attributes lines 18-19, 22-25, 28-37, and 42 of `updateEnvFile()` to `4c9c826c`; only the single `newLine = `${key}="${value}"`` line was later touched by `8c1989e3`.
Blame also attributes the `.env` persistence call sites to `4c9c826c`: `setUserTokens()` lines 195-199, `getOrRefreshAppAccessToken()` lines 241-244, and `refreshUserToken()` lines 367, 369-382.

At the parent commit `a00b6c592895a6ed9da8b8ba72e5bf43db0a9060`, `src/auth/oauth.ts` contains no `updateEnvFile()` helper and no automatic writes of refreshed tokens to `.env`; it only kept tokens in memory or told the user to update `.env` manually.

bug_commits.author: George Peden
bug_commits.message: Fix: quote token values in .env to prevent # truncation

llm_verdict.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced the specific line of code containing the vulnerable regex pattern and the insecure string concatenation logic. By dynamically constructing a RegExp using user-provided keys without sanitization, it created the environment variable injection vulnerability.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: The application used insecure regular expressions to update the .env file, allowing an attacker to inject arbitrary environment variables or manipulate file structure by providing crafted input keys or values.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: new RegExp(`^(#\\s*)?${key}=.*$`, 'gm')
llm_verdict.causal_chain: The commit introduced the `new RegExp` constructor using an unsanitized `key` variable, which allows an attacker to break out of the intended line match and inject arbitrary content into the .env file.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The fix removes the entire manual `.env` update routine and replaces it with `dotenv.parse` + `dotenv-stringify`, so the security-relevant vulnerable pattern is the hand-built regex/string serialization in `updateEnvFile()`. In the pre-fix file, blame shows that the regex loop and the replace/append logic came from commit `4c9c826c`, while `8c1989e3` only changed one line from `key=value` to `key="value"` to address `#` truncation. Checking the parent state at `1c22d404` confirms the same unsafe manual serialization already existed before `8c1989e3`, so this commit did not introduce the environment-variable injection issue; it merely added an insufficient quoting mitigation.
evidence: `blame_lines` on `src/auth/oauth.ts` at the pre-fix state (`8c1989e3`) attributes lines 22-25 and 28-34 of `updateEnvFile()` to `4c9c826c`; only line 26 (`const newLine = `${key}="${value}"`;`) is from `8c1989e3`.
`get_file_at_commit` for the suspected BIC's parent `1c22d4041eb8cae1f719ac8230e42038279623a2` shows the same manual `.env` writing logic already existed, with `const newLine = `${key}=${value}`;`.
`get_commit_diff` for `8c1989e3` shows a one-line change: `key=value` -> `key="value"`, matching the commit message about preventing `#` truncation rather than introducing the serializer itself.

`get_commit_diff`/`get_commit_info` for `4c9c826c6fc8b64a20e948cb46fefdda42d5244d` show that commit originally added `updateEnvFile()` and the token-persistence behavior that writes token values back into `.env`.

cves.description: zae-limiter: DynamoDB hot partition throttling enables per-entity Denial of Service
cves.how_introduced: The commit introduced a mechanism that relied on detecting WCU exhaustion to trigger shard doubling; however, because the initial state was not sharded, the system remained vulnerable to hot partitions during the period before the shard count was successfully increased, allowing a single entity to exhaust the WCU of the underlying DynamoDB partition.
cves.root_cause: The rate limiter used a single DynamoDB partition key for high-traffic entities, leading to 'hot partitions' and WCU exhaustion. This allowed a single high-volume entity to throttle the entire database partition, causing a Denial of Service for other entities sharing that partition.

bug_commits.author: Patrick Sodré
bug_commits.message: ✨ feat(limiter): add shard selection, retry on exhaustion, and shard doubling

llm_verdict.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced the `_is_wcu_exhausted` logic and the associated shard-doubling mechanism, which implemented the flawed strategy of using a single DynamoDB partition key for rate-limit buckets. This logic failed to account for the fact that high-traffic entities would still hit the same partition key before the shard count could be effectively increased, leading to the described hot partition DoS.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: The rate limiter used a single DynamoDB partition key for high-traffic entities, leading to 'hot partitions' and WCU exhaustion. This allowed a single high-volume entity to throttle the entire database partition, causing a Denial of Service for other entities sharing that partition.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: DynamoDB partition keys mapped directly to rate-limit buckets without sharding, leading to write throughput bottlenecks on single partition keys.
llm_verdict.causal_chain: The commit introduced a mechanism that relied on detecting WCU exhaustion to trigger shard doubling; however, because the initial state was not sharded, the system remained vulnerable to hot partitions during the period before the shard count was successfully increased, allowing a single entity to exhaust the WCU of the underlying DynamoDB partition.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: Blame on the pre-fix mainline state (`a586b810`, the state before the later hardening commits were merged) shows the `_is_wcu_exhausted` / shard-retry logic in `src/zae_limiter/limiter.py` was authored by `9f66c42f`. But `9f66c42f` added shard selection, retry, and shard-count doubling on top of a parent state that had no limiter-side sharding at all, so speculative writes still concentrated on shard 0 and the hot-partition DoS condition already existed before this commit. The later fix (`481ce44` / `e67d0112`) hardens that mitigation by adding explicit `SpeculativeFailureReason` classification and restricting retries, which is better characterized as strengthening an existing security control than introducing the vulnerability.
evidence: `blame_lines` on `a586b810:src/zae_limiter/limiter.py:738-750` attributes the shard-doubling and shard-retry block directly to `9f66c42f`.
`blame_lines` on `a586b810:src/zae_limiter/limiter.py:1021-1055` attributes `_is_wcu_exhausted` and `_retry_on_other_shard` implementation to `9f66c42f` (only docstring edits came later).

`get_file_at_commit` for `9f66c42f^` (`e17e3a51`) shows `_try_speculative_acquire` had no shard-doubling or retry path at all; it simply called `_check_speculative_failure` and returned, meaning no limiter-side sharding mitigation existed yet.

Blame on the last vulnerable state before the fixes (using commit `23513a0`, which only made minor boto3 type-value cleanup) attributes the core proactive-sharding logic and the shard-propagation loop/condition to `6bcd2606`. The BIC's parent `199c04fd` has no `try_proactive_shard()`/`propagate_shard_count()` at all, so these vulnerable paths were newly introduced by `6bcd2606`; later fixes rewrote them to use token headroom and to create full shard items rather than only `SET shard_count` on targets.

`git blame` at `23513a0` attributes `try_proactive_shard()` core logic (`wcu_tc_delta / wcu_capacity_milli`, threshold 0.8, lines 632-687) to `6bcd2606`; only minor `ExpressionAttributeValues` syntax was changed later by `23513a0`.

`git blame` at `23513a0` attributes `propagate_shard_count()`'s `for target_shard in range(1, new_count)` + `UpdateExpression='SET shard_count = :new'` + `attribute_not_exists(shard_count) OR shard_count < :new` logic (lines 740-749) to `6bcd2606`.

At `199c04fd` (the BIC parent), `src/zae_limiter_aggregator/processor.py` has no proactive sharding loop after refill and no `try_proactive_shard()` / `propagate_shard_count()` definitions, confirming the vulnerable code did not predate `6bcd2606`.

The fix commit rewrites those exact BIC-added paths: it replaces batch-size-dependent `wcu_tc_delta` signaling with token-level `wcu_tk_milli`, and replaces the single `UpdateItem` propagation loop with logic that updates only existing shards and `PutItem`s full cloned records for new shards.

cves.description: Keygraph Shannon contains a hard-coded API key in its router configuration that, when the router component is enabled and exposed, allows network attackers to authenticate using the publicly known static key. An attacker able to reach the router port can proxy requests through the Shannon instance using the victim’s configured upstream provider API credentials, resulting in unauthorized API usage and potential disclosure of proxied request and response data. This vulnerability's general exploita
cves.how_introduced: The blamed commit added `env: { ...process.env, ... }` to the MCP server configuration, which caused the subprocess to inherit all environment variables from the parent process, including sensitive API keys that were subsequently exposed through the router service.
cves.root_cause: The application exposes a router service on all network interfaces with a hard-coded API key, allowing remote attackers to authenticate and proxy requests.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The fix in 023cc953 patches a path-traversal bug in processIncludes(): it replaces path.join(baseDir, match[1]) with path.resolve(...) and adds a guard rejecting include paths that escape baseDir before fs.readFile. The suspected BIC 9be2e71 is the commit that introduced both the new processIncludes() function and its unsafe path.join+readFile logic, and it also wired that function into loadPrompt() for @include processing. Although blame on the later TypeScript file is partially attributed to the migration commit 3ac07a47, history shows that migration and later file moves preserved the same logic without changing behavior; before 9be2e71 there was no processIncludes function and no @include support at all.
evidence: Fix diff 023cc953 in src/services/prompt-manager.ts adds path.resolve(baseDir, match[1]) and a startsWith(resolvedBase + path.sep) containment check before fs.readFile, showing the vulnerable pattern was missing path validation in processIncludes().

BIC 9be2e71 diff in src/prompts/prompt-manager.js explicitly adds processIncludes() with `const includePath = path.join(baseDir, match[1]); const sharedContent = await fs.readFile(includePath, 'utf8');` and adds the call `template = await processIncludes(template, promptsDir);`.

State before the BIC (27334a4, src/prompts/prompt-manager.js) shows no processIncludes() function; repository search at that commit found no `processIncludes` and no `@include` usage, so the vulnerable feature did not exist before 9be2e71.

Blame on the pre-migration JS file (7c2edeb, lines 93-101) attributes the security-relevant lines, including `const includePath = path.join(baseDir, match[1]);`, directly to 9be2e71; later commits 3ac07a47 (TypeScript migration) and b208949 (move to src/services/) only carried the same logic forward.

cves.description: ZeptoClaw: Generic webhook channel trusts caller-supplied identity fields; allowlist is checked against untrusted payload data
cves.how_introduced: By creating the `WebhookChannel` and defining its payload structure to inherently trust the `sender` and `chat_id` fields provided by unauthenticated external callers, this commit established the root vulnerability of identity spoofing.
cves.root_cause: The webhook channel trusted identity fields like sender and chat_id directly from the request payload without cryptographic verification, allowing external callers to impersonate any identity by providing matching values in the JSON body.

In the pre-fix state, `WebhookPayload` required caller-supplied `sender`/`chat_id`, `handle_connection()` checked the allowlist against `payload.sender`, and then constructed `InboundMessage` from those same untrusted fields; git blame on the pre-fix lines attributes those exact statements to 2c9deef. The file did not exist before that commit, and searches at the earlier commit 44227c found no webhook channel implementation, so this was not a refactor or blame drift case.
evidence: `get_commit_diff(2c9deef, src/channels/webhook.rs)` shows the commit created `src/channels/webhook.rs` from `/dev/null`, including the request format requiring `sender` and `chat_id` in the JSON body.

At fix parent 3ae2add, `blame_lines` attributes `WebhookPayload { message, sender, chat_id }` (lines 126-133) and the allowlist/inbound-message use of `payload.sender`/`payload.chat_id` (lines 420-435) to commit 2c9deef.

`get_file_at_commit(3ae2add, src/channels/webhook.rs)` shows the vulnerable logic: `if !base_config.is_allowed(&payload.sender)` followed by `InboundMessage::new("webhook", payload.sender.trim(), payload.chat_id.trim(), ...)`.

`get_file_at_commit(44227c, src/channels/webhook.rs)` fails because the file did not exist before 2c9deef, and `search_code_in_repo` at 44227c found no webhook-channel matches, confirming the vulnerable code path was introduced by this commit.

cves.description: zae-limiter: DynamoDB hot partition throttling enables per-entity Denial of Service
cves.how_introduced: The commit introduced a mechanism that relied on detecting WCU exhaustion to trigger shard doubling; however, because the initial state was not sharded, the system remained vulnerable to hot partitions during the period before the shard count was successfully increased, allowing a single entity to exhaust the WCU of the underlying DynamoDB partition.
cves.root_cause: The rate limiter used a single DynamoDB partition key for high-traffic entities, leading to 'hot partitions' and WCU exhaustion. This allowed a single high-volume entity to throttle the entire database partition, causing a Denial of Service for other entities sharing that partition.

bug_commits.message: ✨ feat(limiter): add shard selection, retry on exhaustion, and shard doubling

llm_verdict.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced the `_is_wcu_exhausted` logic and the associated shard-doubling mechanism, which implemented the flawed strategy of using a single DynamoDB partition key for rate-limit buckets. This logic failed to account for the fact that high-traffic entities would still hit the same partition key before the shard count could be effectively increased, leading to the described hot partition DoS.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: The rate limiter used a single DynamoDB partition key for high-traffic entities, leading to 'hot partitions' and WCU exhaustion. This allowed a single high-volume entity to throttle the entire database partition, causing a Denial of Service for other entities sharing that partition.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: DynamoDB partition keys mapped directly to rate-limit buckets without sharding, leading to write throughput bottlenecks on single partition keys.

cves.description: OpenClaw versions prior to 2026.2.2 contain a vulnerability in the gateway WebSocket connect handshake in which it allows skipping device identity checks when auth.token is present but not validated. Attackers can connect to the gateway without providing device identity or pairing by exploiting the presence check instead of validation, potentially gaining operator access in vulnerable deployments.
cves.how_introduced: The blamed commit added a condition `hasTokenAuth = !!connectParams.auth?.token` and modified the device check to `if (!device && !hasTokenAuth)`. This allowed connections with an unvalidated token to bypass the device identity requirement, leading to the authentication bypass vulnerability.
cves.root_cause: The gateway connection logic incorrectly allowed skipping device identity checks if an authentication token was present, even if that token had not yet been validated.

bug_commits.author: David Hurley
bug_commits.message: fix: allow token auth to bypass device identity requirement

llm_verdict.reasoning: The blamed commit explicitly introduces the logic to skip device identity checks if a token is present (`!device && !hasTokenAuth`). This directly causes the vulnerability where device identity is bypassed before the token is actually validated.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: The gateway connection logic incorrectly allowed skipping device identity checks if an authentication token was present, even if that token had not yet been validated.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: if (auth.token && !isTokenValidated) { skipDeviceIdentityCheck(); }
llm_verdict.causal_chain: The blamed commit added a condition `hasTokenAuth = !!connectParams.auth?.token` and modified the device check to `if (!device && !hasTokenAuth)`. This allowed connections with an unvalidated token to bypass the device identity requirement, leading to the authentication bypass vulnerability.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The fix addresses a presence-vs-validation bug: it moves authorizeGatewayConnect ahead of the device-bypass decision and replaces raw `hasTokenAuth`/`hasSharedAuth` checks with validated auth results (`sharedAuthOk`). Although blame on the pre-fix lines points to later commits f76e3c14192704d8d5e0af30a360d222283cd91f and e6bdffe568175e2b718e7611f73e191a9e1f771a, those commits only refactored/extended the same bypass logic and preserved the vulnerable `... : hasTokenAuth` fallback. The suspected commit 079af0d0b02ca2c722f90b6c4e38e27ba16227b4 is where the no-device bypass was first introduced: it changed `if (!device)` to `if (!device && !hasTokenAuth)` and added the `&& device` guard to token verification, explicitly allowing device-less connections based solely on token presence. In the prior file state (409a16060b9c41b08d5b73e0f5ae1da346d156b4), a missing device always caused immediate rejection, so this vulnerability did not exist before 079af0d0.
evidence: BIC diff 079af0d0 introduced `const hasTokenAuth = !!connectParams.auth?.token; if (!device && !hasTokenAuth)`, replacing the previous unconditional `if (!device)` rejection.
BIC also changed device-token verification to `if (!authOk && connectParams.auth?.token && device)`, consistent with permitting a no-device path before auth validation.
File at 409a16060b9c41b08d5b73e0f5ae1da346d156b4 shows no bypass existed before: missing `device` always returned `device identity required` before authorizeGatewayConnect ran.

Pre-fix blame points to f76e3c.../e6bdffe..., but their diffs show they preserved the original `hasTokenAuth` bypass (`... : hasTokenAuth`) while adding control-ui-specific wrappers, indicating blame drift/refactor rather than original introduction.
Fix fe81b1d7 removes the presence-based bypass by computing validated `authResult/sharedAuthOk` before `if (!device)` and using that validated result instead of raw token presence.

cves.description: ZeptoClaw: Path boundary checks bypass via symlink, TOCTOU, and hardlink
cves.how_introduced: The blamed commit updated `resolve_path` to use `validate_path_in_workspace` and return a safe path string, but failed to protect against race conditions where the filesystem structure changes between validation and use. By not re-validating paths right before read/write operations, it introduced the TOCTOU and symlink bypass vulnerabilities.
cves.root_cause: The webhook channel trusted identity fields like sender and chat_id directly from the request payload without cryptographic verification, allowing external callers to impersonate any identity by providing matching values in the JSON body.

bug_commits.message: feat: add deny_by_default sender allowlist mode (#22)

llm_verdict.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced logic for 'deny_by_default' and allowlist filtering based on legitimate user IDs extracted from the Telegram SDK's 'Message' object. The vulnerability involves trusting user-supplied identity fields in a custom webhook payload, whereas the blamed commit focuses on handling Telegram Bot API message structures correctly.

llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: pub struct WebhookPayload { pub sender: String, pub chat_id: String, ... }

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The Telegram portion of the fix hardens inbound auth by stopping unconditional matching of mutable Telegram usernames in `allow_from`; the security-relevant change is the new `telegram_allowlist_allows(..., allow_usernames)` gate. In the pre-fix file, git blame shows the actual username-matching lines (`|| (!username.is_empty() && allowlist.iter().any(...))`) were introduced by commit `d6f0c8d2501bed3e0cea4c7a0428b82e36cbc84b`, not by `b276347f1a14f2c3c502d58dd3b51b5076c59fbc`. The suspected BIC only added `deny_by_default` handling around an existing numeric-ID allowlist check, which is a security control later strengthened by the fix rather than the source of the vulnerability.
evidence: At fix-parent `37e2262dfd1d252c8b7c760a9eca7fee02c34410`, blame on `src/channels/telegram.rs:435-442` attributes the username allowlist matching logic to `d6f0c8d2501bed3e0cea4c7a0428b82e36cbc84b`; only the surrounding `deny_by_default` scaffolding at lines 431-434/443-444 blames to `b276347f...`.

`b276347f` diff for `src/channels/telegram.rs` adds only `deny_by_default` propagation and `let allowed = if allowlist.is_empty() { !deny_by_default } else { allowlist.contains(&user_id) };` — no username handling.
File state before/at the BIC confirms this: before BIC (`825bae0f...` / earlier `c5c03e8d...`) Telegram used numeric-ID-only allowlist checks, and at BIC it still remained numeric-only.
Commit `d6f0c8d2501bed3e0cea4c7a0428b82e36cbc84b` explicitly says 'Bonus: allow_from now also accepts usernames' and its diff adds the exact username-comparison code later restricted by the fix.

In the pre-fix file (`3ae2add...`), the vulnerable `POST` branch still immediately wrote `HTTP_200_OK` and parsed/published the body with no authenticity check, and `blame_lines` attributes those lines directly to `061066f7`. `git_log` shows `src/channels/whatsapp_cloud.rs` was created in `061066f7` and has no earlier history, while the later wiring commit `5ddaece` only registered/exported the channel and did not change the vulnerable handler logic.

`get_file_at_commit` for `061066f761ebe337c0b480a24560e5f1b9126280` shows the original code: `"POST" => { // Always respond 200 immediately ... let _ = stream.write_all(HTTP_200_OK.as_bytes()).await; ... serde_json::from_str(&request.body) ... }`, with no signature validation.
`git_log` for `src/channels/whatsapp_cloud.rs` shows the earliest commit on the file is `061066f7`, confirming the vulnerable code did not predate that commit.

`get_commit_info` for `061066f7` shows it added `src/channels/whatsapp_cloud.rs` as a new file; `search_code_in_repo` at earlier commit `14fca49...` finds no `whatsapp_cloud` references, indicating the implementation was introduced here.

`get_commit_info`/diff for `5ddaece5b75c4284e1e7724aae8d613eb601d7a1` shows that later commit only wired the channel into `mod.rs`/`factory.rs`; it did not modify `src/channels/whatsapp_cloud.rs`, so the vulnerable logic itself still originates from `061066f7`.

llm_verdict.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced the workspace path validation logic that performs a single upfront validation without subsequent checks. This design left the filesystem operations vulnerable to TOCTOU (Time-of-Check to Time-of-Use) and symlink/hardlink attacks, which the fix commit addresses by adding revalidation immediately before I/O operations.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: The webhook channel trusted identity fields like sender and chat_id directly from the request payload without cryptographic verification, allowing external callers to impersonate any identity by providing matching values in the JSON body.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: pub struct WebhookPayload { pub sender: String, pub chat_id: String, ... }
llm_verdict.causal_chain: The blamed commit updated `resolve_path` to use `validate_path_in_workspace` and return a safe path string, but failed to protect against race conditions where the filesystem structure changes between validation and use. By not re-validating paths right before read/write operations, it introduced the TOCTOU and symlink bypass vulnerabilities.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The suspected commit 120e5dd2 added `validate_path_in_workspace` to `resolve_path` and changed callers to propagate `Result`, i.e. it introduced path-traversal protection rather than the underlying unsafe file I/O pattern. The pre-BIC file at 89d3202f already had the same read/write/list/edit flows performing filesystem operations immediately after path resolution with no revalidation or hardlink checks, and blame on the pre-fix file shows most of those I/O lines still trace to 89d3202f. Additionally, the symlink-specific buggy `current.exists()` logic fixed in `src/security/path.rs` traces to later commit 7a2db595, not to 120e5dd2. So this looks like an insufficient mitigation later strengthened by the fix, not the true bug-introducing commit for the CVE.
evidence: `git log src/tools/filesystem.rs` shows the file was created in 89d3202f (`feat(rust): implement filesystem and shell tools`), before 120e5dd2.

At 89d3202f, `src/tools/filesystem.rs` already had `resolve_path(...)` followed directly by `tokio::fs::read_to_string`, `create_dir_all` + `tokio::fs::write`, `read_dir`, and edit read/write flows, with no `revalidate_path` or hardlink protection.

The 120e5dd2 diff only adds `use crate::security::validate_path_in_workspace;`, rewrites `resolve_path` to validate within the workspace, and changes call sites from `resolve_path(path, ctx)` to `resolve_path(path, ctx)?`—a security improvement, not new unsafe I/O logic.

Blame on the pre-fix file (5dad36b9) attributes key unsafe operations to earlier commits: e.g. write/create-dir/edit I/O lines mostly trace to 89d3202f, while 120e5dd2 mainly owns the `let full_path = resolve_path(path, ctx)?;` lines.

For the symlink aspect, blame on `src/security/path.rs` at the pre-fix state attributes the vulnerable `if current.exists()` / `canonicalize()` symlink check to 7a2db595, a later commit than 120e5dd2.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: 89d3202f did create `src/tools/filesystem.rs`, but in that commit there was no workspace-boundary validation layer at all: `resolve_path()` simply passed absolute paths through or joined relative paths, and repository search at 89d shows no `validate_path_in_workspace`/symlink-check code. The specific CVE fixed in `f50c17e` is a bypass of later-added boundary checks: commit `120e5dd2` introduced `validate_path_in_workspace` into `filesystem.rs`, and blame on the pre-fix `let full_path = resolve_path(path, ctx)?;` lines points to `120e5dd2`, not 89d. The dangling-symlink logic in `src/security/path.rs` was introduced later as well (`7a2db595`/`2b56bc8a`). So while 89d authored the original file operations, it did not author the specific boundary-check implementation that this fix hardens.
evidence: `git_log src/tools/filesystem.rs` shows the file was first added in `89d3202f`; `get_file_at_commit` for parent `157d1b1d` reports the file did not exist before that commit.

At `89d3202f`, `src/tools/filesystem.rs` had `resolve_path()` that returned absolute paths as-is or joined relative paths to the workspace; `search_code_in_repo` at that commit found no `validate_path_in_workspace` or `check_symlink_escape` references.

Commit `120e5dd2` (`feat(tools): add path traversal protection to filesystem tools`) changed `resolve_path` to call `validate_path_in_workspace` and updated read/write/list/edit to use `resolve_path(path, ctx)?`, creating the later 'validate then I/O' pattern.

`blame_lines` on pre-fix `src/tools/filesystem.rs` attribute the security-relevant `let full_path = resolve_path(path, ctx)?;` lines to `120e5dd2`; `blame_lines` on pre-fix `src/security/path.rs` attribute `check_symlink_escape` to `7a2db595`, not `89d3202f`.

bug_commits.author: qhkmdev90
bug_commits.message: feat(tools): add pdf_read tool, feature-gated with --features tool-pdf

llm_verdict.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced the PdfReadTool implementation, which lacked the re-validation logic required to prevent the TOCTOU vulnerability identified in the fix. By introducing the tool without a check immediately prior to the I/O operation, it established the vulnerable pattern that was later addressed by the fix.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: The webhook channel trusted identity fields like sender and chat_id directly from the request payload without cryptographic verification, allowing external callers to impersonate any identity by providing matching values in the JSON body.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: pub struct WebhookPayload { pub sender: String, pub chat_id: String, ... }
llm_verdict.causal_chain: The introduction of PdfReadTool in commit fe70dcd422ad created a window of time between the initial file validation and the actual file read operation. Because no re-validation was performed, a malicious actor could replace the file with a symbolic link during this window, leading to the TOCTOU vulnerability fixed in f50c17e11ae3.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The fix in `src/tools/pdf_read.rs` adds `revalidate_path()` inside `PdfReadTool::resolve_path()` immediately before filesystem use, closing the TOCTOU window between `validate_path_in_workspace()` and later `exists()/metadata/load` operations. Blame on the pre-fix version shows the vulnerable `resolve_path` lines (45-55) were introduced by `51bc07a02484ddfd2ec9c7f382dc43f829a9df86`. History also shows `src/tools/pdf_read.rs` did not exist before that commit, and the only intermediate change (`5dad36b...`) touched `ToolOutput` plumbing rather than the path-validation logic. So this commit authored the exact vulnerable code path later patched by the fix.
evidence: Fix diff for `f50c17e...` on `src/tools/pdf_read.rs` adds `revalidate_path(safe.as_path(), &self.workspace)?;` in `resolve_path`, explicitly described in the commit message as the TOCTOU mitigation.

`blame_lines` on pre-fix `src/tools/pdf_read.rs` (lines 45-55 at `d8d9cb267251f07205cf4418eb0518eb35982c2b`) attributes the vulnerable `resolve_path` body to `51bc07a0`, including `let safe = validate_path_in_workspace(...)` and the subsequent `exists()` check without revalidation.
`get_commit_diff` for `51bc07a...` shows it created `src/tools/pdf_read.rs` and introduced `resolve_path` with the vulnerable pattern.
`get_file_at_commit` for `24386e4b289c6e247e0b32bf5bae1814cdb024ae` reports `src/tools/pdf_read.rs` did not exist before `51bc07a...`, ruling out pre-existing code or a pure move/rename.
`get_commit_diff` for intermediate commit `5dad36b...` shows only `ToolOutput`/return-type changes in `pdf_read.rs`, not the security-relevant path-validation logic.

bug_commits.author: qhkmdev90
bug_commits.message: feat(tools): add transcribe tool for voice-to-text via Groq Whisper

llm_verdict.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced the `TranscribeTool` (src/tools/transcribe.rs), which performed file I/O based on a resolved path without sufficient protection against TOCTOU (Time-of-Check to Time-of-Use) attacks. The fix commit directly addresses this by adding `revalidate_path` inside the tool's implementation created by the blamed commit.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: The webhook channel trusted identity fields like sender and chat_id directly from the request payload without cryptographic verification, allowing external callers to impersonate any identity by providing matching values in the JSON body.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: pub struct WebhookPayload { pub sender: String, pub chat_id: String, ... }
llm_verdict.causal_chain: 1. The blamed commit adds the 'TranscribeTool' and its implementation in src/tools/transcribe.rs. 2. This implementation performs a file transcription operation using a path resolved from input. 3. The implementation lacks a re-validation step immediately before the I/O operation, creating a TOCTOU window where a symlink or file could be swapped after initial validation. 4. This missing security check constitutes the vulnerability addressed in the fix.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The fix in f50c17 addresses a TOCTOU issue by adding `revalidate_path(...)` immediately before `self.transcribe_file(&resolved).await` in `TranscribeTool::execute`, so the security-relevant vulnerable pattern is the original validate-then-use gap. Blame on the pre-fix file state attributes the path-resolution and direct `transcribe_file(&resolved)` call to 5dad36b9, and file history shows `src/tools/transcribe.rs` was introduced in that commit and only later modified by the fix. Although 5dad already performed initial workspace validation, it also created the entire new transcribe code path containing the missing pre-I/O revalidation, so this is a new vulnerable implementation rather than merely a later-strengthened mitigation.
evidence: Fix diff for `src/tools/transcribe.rs` adds `revalidate_path(Path::new(&resolved), ws)` right before `match self.transcribe_file(&resolved).await`, explicitly closing the TOCTOU window.

`blame_lines` on commit 5dad36b962af6258bb059b30bd1b4b5bac3e7442 for lines 155-203 attributes the vulnerable `execute` logic, including `validate_path_in_workspace(...)` and `self.transcribe_file(&resolved).await`, to 5dad36b9.
`git_log` for `src/tools/transcribe.rs` shows only two commits: its introduction in 5dad36b962af6258bb059b30bd1b4b5bac3e7442 and the hardening fix in f50c17e11ae3e2d40c96730abac41974ef2ee2a8.

`get_commit_diff` for 5dad shows `src/tools/transcribe.rs` as `new file mode`; `get_file_at_commit` at earlier commit e686608e08dad6cf9b7c6368d47feb1e634d7dbe fails because `src/tools/transcribe.rs` did not exist yet.

cves.description: OpenClaw: Native prompt image auto-load did not honor tools.fs.workspaceOnly in sandboxed runs
cves.how_introduced: By adding the ability to load images from paths detected in prompts without validating them against the workspace boundary, this commit allowed the system to read and ingest arbitrary files outside the intended directory, leading to the path traversal vulnerability.
cves.root_cause: The native prompt image auto-load feature did not enforce the `tools.fs.workspaceOnly` restriction, allowing out-of-workspace files to be read and ingested as vision input.

bug_commits.message: feat: native image injection for vision-capable models

llm_verdict.vuln_description: The native prompt image auto-load feature did not enforce the `tools.fs.workspaceOnly` restriction, allowing out-of-workspace files to be read and ingested as vision input.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: Loading image files from arbitrary paths without validating them against the allowed workspace directory boundary.
llm_verdict.causal_chain: By adding the ability to load images from paths detected in prompts without validating them against the workspace boundary, this commit allowed the system to read and ingest arbitrary files outside the intended directory, leading to the path traversal vulnerability.

cves.description: OpenClaw has ReDoS and regex injection via unescaped Feishu mention metadata in RegExp construction
cves.how_introduced: The commit introduced the stripBotMention function, which directly passes user-controlled mention.name and mention.key into the RegExp constructor without escaping. This allows an attacker to inject regex metacharacters, causing the vulnerability.
cves.root_cause: The application constructs a regular expression using unescaped user-controlled metadata (mention names and keys) from Feishu messages. This allows an attacker to inject regex metacharacters, potentially leading to Regular Expression Denial of Service (ReDoS) or incorrect message parsing.

bug_commits.author: Yifeng Wang
bug_commits.message: feat(feishu): replace built-in SDK with community plugin

llm_verdict.reasoning: The blamed commit is the initial creation of the bot.ts file, which introduced the entire Feishu bot implementation. This includes the stripBotMention function that constructs regular expressions using unescaped mention metadata, leading to the ReDoS/regex injection vulnerability.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: The application constructs a regular expression using unescaped user-controlled metadata (mention names and keys) from Feishu messages. This allows an attacker to inject regex metacharacters, potentially leading to Regular Expression Denial of Service (ReDoS) or incorrect message parsing.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: new RegExp(mention.name) or similar construction using unescaped mention metadata
llm_verdict.causal_chain: The commit introduced the stripBotMention function, which directly passes user-controlled mention.name and mention.key into the RegExp constructor without escaping. This allows an attacker to inject regex metacharacters, causing the vulnerability.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The fix in 7e67ab75 adds `escapeRegExp()` and applies it exactly where `stripBotMention()` was previously constructing `new RegExp()` from untrusted `mention.name` and `mention.key`, which is the vulnerable regex-injection/ReDoS pattern. In the pre-fix file state, git blame attributes those two vulnerable lines directly to 2267d58afcc70fe19408b8f0dce108c340f3426d. History also shows `extensions/feishu/src/bot.ts` was created in 2267d58 (from `/dev/null`), so this was not a move-only refactor; the prior Feishu implementation in `src/feishu/message.ts` used plain `text.replace(mention.key, "")` instead of `new RegExp(...)`, meaning the specific vulnerable pattern was newly introduced by 2267d58.
evidence: Fix diff 7e67ab75 patches only `extensions/feishu/src/bot.ts` by adding `escapeRegExp()` and changing `new RegExp(`@${mention.name}\\s*`, 'g')` / `new RegExp(mention.key, 'g')` to escaped forms.
Pre-fix file content at 0e85380 shows `stripBotMention()` still had the raw regex constructions on mention metadata.
`blame_lines` on pre-fix `extensions/feishu/src/bot.ts` attributes the vulnerable lines (209-210) to commit 2267d58afcc70fe19408b8f0dce108c340f3426d.
`git_log` for `extensions/feishu/src/bot.ts` shows the file history starts at 2267d58, and the 2267 diff creates the file from `/dev/null`.

At pre-BIC commit 0223416, `extensions/feishu/src/bot.ts` did not exist, and the older Feishu code in `src/feishu/message.ts` removed mentions with `text.replace(mention.key, "").trim()` rather than constructing a regex from mention metadata.

cves.description: OpenClaw: Feishu webhook mode accepted forged events when only `verificationToken` was configured
cves.how_introduced: The blamed commit initialized the `feishuPlugin` configuration schema with `encryptKey` defined as a simple `{ type: 'string' }` object. This lack of proper input validation/masking meant the application did not enforce the security requirement of an encryption key, allowing the system to fall back to using only the `verificationToken` for authentication, thereby creating the opportunity for forged events.
cves.root_cause: The application incorrectly allowed Feishu webhooks to be authenticated using only a verification token, bypassing mandatory encryption key validation which is required to prevent forged events.

bug_commits.author: Yifeng Wang
bug_commits.message: feat(feishu): replace built-in SDK with community plugin

llm_verdict.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced the structural configuration for the Feishu plugin, where `encryptKey` was defined as a simple string type rather than a secret-masked input. This implementation detail contributed to the vulnerability by not properly treating the encryption key as a sensitive, mandatory credential, which allowed the system to operate (improperly) without enforcing its presence or correct validation during webhook processing.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: The application incorrectly allowed Feishu webhooks to be authenticated using only a verification token, bypassing mandatory encryption key validation which is required to prevent forged events.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: Verification of Feishu webhook events based solely on a `verificationToken` without enforcing the presence and validation of an `encryptKey`.
llm_verdict.causal_chain: The blamed commit initialized the `feishuPlugin` configuration schema with `encryptKey` defined as a simple `{ type: 'string' }` object. This lack of proper input validation/masking meant the application did not enforce the security requirement of an encryption key, allowing the system to fall back to using only the `verificationToken` for authentication, thereby creating the opportunity for forged events.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: 2267d58 did author the original `encryptKey: { type: "string" }` schema stub in `extensions/feishu/src/channel.ts`, but at that point OpenClaw's Feishu monitor explicitly did not implement webhook mode, so the product could not yet accept forged webhook events. The exploitable behavior arrived later: 5c2cb6c implemented `monitorWebhook(...)`, a23e0d added only a `verificationToken` requirement, and later secret-input commits (806803b / b7589b32) made `verificationToken` secret-aware while leaving `encryptKey` unresolved as a plain string. So the blamed 2267 commit laid groundwork, but it did not introduce the vulnerable webhook-acceptance behavior fixed by 7844bc89.
evidence: At 2267d58, `extensions/feishu/src/monitor.ts` ends webhook startup with `log("feishu: webhook mode not implemented in monitor, use HTTP server directly")`, showing built-in webhook mode was not implemented yet.

Commit 5c2cb6c (`feat(feishu): sync community contributions...`) added the actual built-in webhook server: `monitorWebhook(...)` with `server.on("request", Lark.adaptDefault(path, eventDispatcher, { autoChallenge: true }))`.

In the pre-fix state, the security-enforcement lines blame to later commits, not 2267: `monitor.account.ts` only required `verificationToken` (blame f46bd2e / earlier a23e0d lineage), and `config-schema.ts` webhook validation likewise required only `verificationToken` (blame a23e0d / 806803b).

The exact schema asymmetry fixed in `channel.ts` was not created solely by 2267: blame on pre-fix `channel.ts` shows `encryptKey` remained from 2267, but `verificationToken` was converted to `secretInputJsonSchema` later by 806803b, which is what created the top-level SecretInput mismatch fixed in 7844bc89.

cves.description: OpenClaw has a Feishu allowFrom authorization bypass via display-name collision
cves.how_introduced: 1. The blamed commit introduced the Feishu extension and its message handling logic. 2. It implemented authorization checks via `resolveFeishuAllowlistMatch` using only `senderName` and `senderId`. 3. Because `senderName` is mutable and can be spoofed to match an entry in the `allowFrom` list, this implementation directly enabled the authorization bypass.
cves.root_cause: The application allowed authorization based on mutable display names, enabling an attacker to bypass the allowlist by changing their display name to match an allowed user's ID.

bug_commits.author: Yifeng Wang
bug_commits.message: feat(feishu): replace built-in SDK with community plugin

llm_verdict.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced the initial implementation of the Feishu bot, including the `handleFeishuMessage` function and the call to `resolveFeishuAllowlistMatch`. It established the pattern of performing authorization checks using `senderName` (a mutable display name) and `senderId` (OpenID) without including the immutable `user_id`, which is the root cause of the display-name collision vulnerability.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: The application allowed authorization based on mutable display names, enabling an attacker to bypass the allowlist by changing their display name to match an allowed user's ID.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: Checking authorization against mutable user attributes like display names instead of immutable identifiers (e.g., `allowFrom.includes(senderName)`).
llm_verdict.causal_chain: 1. The blamed commit introduced the Feishu extension and its message handling logic. 2. It implemented authorization checks via `resolveFeishuAllowlistMatch` using only `senderName` and `senderId`. 3. Because `senderName` is mutable and can be spoofed to match an entry in the `allowFrom` list, this implementation directly enabled the authorization bypass.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The fix removes Feishu’s ability to authorize `allowFrom` entries by mutable `senderName` and replaces it with ID-only matching plus additional immutable `senderIds` candidates. The suspected BIC created `extensions/feishu/src/policy.ts` with inline logic that explicitly returned an allowlist match on `senderName`, and it created the `bot.ts` call site that passed `ctx.senderName` into group sender allowlist checks. Pre-BIC Feishu code in `src/feishu/access.ts`/`src/feishu/message.ts` was ID-only (`Feishu IDs are open_id or union_id, no usernames`), so this was not a pre-existing bug merely moved around. A later refactor (`fef86e475b49`) only replaced the inline vulnerable logic with `resolveAllowlistMatchSimple(params)` and did not change behavior, so the underlying vulnerability still traces back to 2267d58.
evidence: Fix commit 4ed87a6 changes `extensions/feishu/src/policy.ts` from `AllowlistMatch<"wildcard"|"id"|"name">` / `resolveAllowlistMatchSimple(params)` to Feishu-specific ID-only matching and adds `senderIds` support; bot.ts now passes `senderUserId` instead of relying on display names.

BIC 2267d58 added `extensions/feishu/src/policy.ts` with inline vulnerable logic: after checking `senderId`, it did `const senderName = params.senderName?.toLowerCase(); if (senderName && allowFrom.includes(senderName)) return { allowed: true, matchSource: "name" };`.

Blame on pre-fix `extensions/feishu/src/bot.ts` lines 600-604 attributes the group sender allowlist call (`isFeishuGroupAllowed(... senderId: ctx.senderOpenId, senderName: ctx.senderName)`) to 2267d58, showing the BIC wired display names into authorization.

Pre-BIC code at 02842bef in deleted `src/feishu/access.ts`/`src/feishu/message.ts` was ID-only: `isSenderAllowed({ allow, senderId })` and comments state `Feishu uses open_id (ou_xxx) or union_id (on_xxx) - no usernames.` No display-name allowlist matching existed there.

The intermediate commit fef86e475b49 is a refactor only: its diff for `extensions/feishu/src/policy.ts` mechanically replaces the BIC’s inline name-matching code with `return resolveAllowlistMatchSimple(params);`, preserving the same vulnerable behavior rather than introducing it.

cves.description: j178/prek-action vulnerable to arbitrary code injection in composite action
cves.how_introduced: The commit added the 'prek-version' input and used '${{ inputs.prek-version }}' directly in the 'run' script block. In GitHub Actions, this allows an attacker to break out of the shell command by providing a malicious payload in the 'prek-version' parameter, leading to arbitrary command execution.

bug_commits.message: Add support for specifying prek-version input

This created a command injection vulnerability that was addressed in the fix commit by passing the input through an environment variable.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: The action directly interpolated user-provided input into shell commands without sanitization, allowing an attacker to inject arbitrary shell commands via the input parameters.

In GitHub Actions, this allows an attacker to break out of the shell command by providing a malicious payload in the 'prek-version' parameter, leading to arbitrary command execution.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The fix specifically removes direct GitHub-expression interpolation from shell `run:` blocks, and one of those security-relevant lines in the pre-fix file (`if [ "${{ inputs.prek-version }}" = "latest" ]; then`) blames to a7c5a005. The parent of a7c5 had no `prek-version` input or related install logic, while a7c5 introduced that feature with unsafe `${{ inputs.prek-version }}` expansion inside bash, which is exactly the pattern the fix replaces with an environment variable. Although the action already had a separate `extra_args` injection sink before this commit, a7c5 still introduced a new vulnerable code path that the fix patches, so it qualifies as a bug-introducing commit for this vulnerability.

The diff for a7c5a005 shows it added the new `prek-version` input and inserted `${{ inputs.prek-version }}` directly into a bash `run:` script (`python -m pip install prek==${{ inputs.prek-version }}`).
The file at a7c5a005's parent commit ce9086fb had no `prek-version` input or version-selection install code, so this vulnerable path did not exist before a7c5.

The fix commit 6b7c6ef replaces direct `${{ inputs.prek-version }}` interpolation with `env: PREK_VERSION` and shell variable references, indicating that this exact pattern was the security issue being fixed.

The diff for 346373c1 shows only an input rename/alias (`extra_args` -> `extra-args`) and replacement of `${{ inputs.extra_args }}` with `${{ inputs.extra_args || inputs.extra-args }}`; no sanitization or logic change that created a new injection class.

cves.description: Label Studio vulnerable to full account takeover by chaining Stored XSS + IDOR in User Profile via custom_hotkeys field
cves.how_introduced: 1. The blamed commit adds the 'custom_hotkeys' field to the User model and creates an API to update it. 2. It introduces a script tag in label_studio/templates/base.html that renders this user-controlled field using the '|safe' filter. 3. This allows an attacker to inject '</script><script>alert(1)</script>' into their custom_hotkeys profile field, leading to Stored XSS.
cves.root_cause: The application fails to properly escape the 'custom_hotkeys' user profile field when rendering it inside a script tag in the base template, allowing an attacker to inject malicious JavaScript via the JSON payload.

bug_commits.author: niklub
bug_commits.message: fix: BROS-114: Fixes for Hotkeys (#7863)

llm_verdict.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced the custom hotkeys feature, including the logic to store 'custom_hotkeys' in the User model and render it in the base template. The diff for 'label_studio/templates/base.html' (though truncated in the snippet) corresponds to the addition of the vulnerable script block using '|safe' without proper character escaping.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: The application fails to properly escape the 'custom_hotkeys' user profile field when rendering it inside a script tag in the base template, allowing an attacker to inject malicious JavaScript via the JSON payload.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: {{ user.custom_hotkeys|json_dumps_ensure_ascii|safe }}
llm_verdict.causal_chain: 1. The blamed commit adds the 'custom_hotkeys' field to the User model and creates an API to update it. 2. It introduces a script tag in label_studio/templates/base.html that renders this user-controlled field using the '|safe' filter. 3. This allows an attacker to inject '</script><script>alert(1)</script>' into their custom_hotkeys profile field, leading to Stored XSS.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The fix patches exactly one security-relevant line in `label_studio/templates/base.html`: it changes `user.custom_hotkeys|json_dumps_ensure_ascii|safe` to add `escape_lt_gt`, which is a classic fix for script-tag breakout / stored XSS when embedding user-controlled JSON into inline JavaScript. Blame on the pre-fix file state attributes that vulnerable line (`var __customHotkeys = ...|safe`) to commit `f0fb90a58`, and the prior file state (`ab7cbf08`) contains no `custom_hotkeys` rendering at all, so this sink did not exist before the suspected BIC. The same commit also introduced the `custom_hotkeys` field in the user model and serializers, making the new attacker-controlled field reachable through the application, so the commit is causally responsible for the vulnerable code path.
evidence: Fix commit `ea2462bf042bbf370b79445d02a205fbe547b505` changes only `label_studio/templates/base.html`, replacing `var __customHotkeys = {{ user.custom_hotkeys|json_dumps_ensure_ascii|safe }};` with `...|escape_lt_gt|safe`, showing the vulnerability is unsafe inline-JS embedding of user-controlled JSON.

Blame on pre-fix state `c9b3c9208a0fbcfebd6a0d3ff28038d22261898b` attributes `label_studio/templates/base.html` line 105 (`var __customHotkeys = {{ user.custom_hotkeys|json_dumps_ensure_ascii|safe }};`) to `f0fb90a58b294d0a2261e6ce59622430d19c7d27`.

`label_studio/templates/base.html` at pre-BIC commit `ab7cbf08f84171ab23eff48a06456ab10855a2d4` has no `custom_hotkeys` / `__customHotkeys` code; the script block only initializes `window.APP_SETTINGS` with existing user fields.

The BIC diff itself adds the vulnerable sink in `base.html` and also adds `custom_hotkeys` to `label_studio/users/models.py` plus `label_studio/users/serializers.py` (field added to `BaseUserSerializer` / update serializer), introducing the new attacker-controlled field and its exposure.
