cves.description: yt-dlp: Arbitrary Command Injection when using the `--netrc-cmd` option
cves.how_introduced: The blamed commit added the `netrc_cmd` execution logic in `_get_netrc_login_info`. By passing the unvalidated `netrc_machine` string into a command executed with `shell=True`, it allowed arbitrary command injection if an attacker could control the `netrc_machine` value.
cves.root_cause: The `_get_netrc_login_info` method fails to validate the `netrc_machine` variable before passing it to a shell command via the `--netrc-cmd` option, allowing an attacker to inject arbitrary shell commands if they can control the machine name.

bug_commits.author: Nicolai Dagestad
bug_commits.message: Add option `--netrc-cmd` (#6682)

llm_verdict.reasoning: The blamed commit introduces the `--netrc-cmd` feature, which formats the `netrc_machine` variable directly into a shell command and executes it using `Popen.run` with `shell=True`. This directly creates the command injection vulnerability that the fix addresses by adding character validation.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: The `_get_netrc_login_info` method fails to validate the `netrc_machine` variable before passing it to a shell command via the `--netrc-cmd` option, allowing an attacker to inject arbitrary shell commands if they can control the machine name.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: subprocess.check_output(f'{cmd} {netrc_machine}', shell=True)
llm_verdict.causal_chain: The blamed commit added the `netrc_cmd` execution logic in `_get_netrc_login_info`. By passing the unvalidated `netrc_machine` string into a command executed with `shell=True`, it allowed arbitrary command injection if an attacker could control the `netrc_machine` value.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The fix’s security-relevant change is the new validation of `netrc_machine` in `_get_netrc_login_info` before it is substituted into `netrc_cmd` and executed with `shell=True`; the `initialize()` change is a secondary guard to avoid reaching that sink unnecessarily. Blame on the pre-fix file shows `db3ad8a67661d7b234a6954d9c6a4a9b1749f5eb` introduced the `--netrc-cmd` path and the shell execution lines (`if cmd: ... Popen.run(cmd, text=True, shell=True, stdout=subprocess.PIPE)`), while a same-day follow-up commit only changed `.format(netrc_machine)` to `.replace('{}', netrc_machine)` without removing the injection risk. The parent of `db3ad8a` had no `netrc_cmd` support and `_get_netrc_login_info` only parsed `.netrc`, so the vulnerable command-execution path did not exist before this commit.
evidence: `db3ad8a` commit message is `Add option --netrc-cmd (#6682)` and its diff adds `cmd = self.get_param('netrc_cmd', '').format(netrc_machine)` / `Popen.run(cmd, text=True, shell=True, stdout=subprocess.PIPE)` in `yt_dlp/extractor/common.py`.
Blame at pre-fix `yt_dlp/extractor/common.py` lines 1390 and 1392-1396 attributes the shell-execution path to `db3ad8a`; only line 1391 was later adjusted by `d7cd97e8` (`Fix bug in db3ad8a...`).

File state at `db3ad8a^` (`8213ce28...`) shows `_get_netrc_login_info` had no `netrc_cmd` branch and only read credentials from `.netrc`, proving the vulnerable sink was newly introduced by `db3ad8a`.

Pre-existing extractors already passed variable `netrc_machine` values into `_get_login_info` (e.g. PornHub `site`, Teachable `site`), and the fix later tightened their regexes, showing `db3ad8a` turned those existing inputs into shell-sensitive data.

cves.description: LangBot has a Cross Site Scripting(XSS) Vulnerability
cves.how_introduced: 1. The blamed commit adds ReactMarkdown to render messages in DebugDialog.tsx. 2. It configures the markdown renderer without a sanitization plugin. 3. This allows raw HTML or malicious script tags within markdown content to be executed in the user's browser, leading to XSS.
cves.root_cause: The vulnerability stems from a dependency-based issue where a third-party package used by LangBot contained a security flaw that permitted arbitrary script injection.

In the pre-fix file state, `git blame` attributes the `ReactMarkdown` + `rehypeRaw` lines in `renderMessageContent` directly to `b634aa48dc27a8df7e7e853914071451db00a04c`. The file at that commit’s parent (`b5d192425e5c717d69317c3b927b780163647510`) had no markdown rendering in `DebugDialog` at all—only plain `whitespace-pre-wrap` rendering—so this was a real introduction of new vulnerable logic, not a refactor or blame drift.

Pre-fix file (`b5b5d499e55ad3f293af3759dc25dba560484c47`) shows `ReactMarkdown` configured with `remarkGfm` and `rehypeRaw` but no sanitization in `renderMessageContent`.
`blame_lines` on pre-fix lines 621-625 attributes the vulnerable `ReactMarkdown`/`rehypeRaw` block to commit `b634aa48d`.

At the BIC parent `b5d192425e5c717d69317c3b927b780163647510`, `DebugDialog.tsx` had no `ReactMarkdown` imports and `renderMessageContent` only rendered `message.message_chain` inside a `whitespace-pre-wrap` div, proving the vulnerable markdown/raw-HTML path was added by `b634aa48`.

cves.description: aws-mcp-server MCP server is vulnerable to command injection. An attacker can craft a prompt that once accessed by the MCP client will run arbitrary commands on the host system.
cves.how_introduced: The commit introduced a validation function that used shlex.split to parse commands, but the application continued to use shell-based execution (subprocess_shell) for the validated strings. Because the validation logic did not strictly sanitize or escape the input for shell context, it allowed attackers to bypass the check using shell metacharacters, leading to command injection.
cves.root_cause: The server failed to properly sanitize or validate user-provided input before passing it to a shell-based command executor, allowing attackers to execute arbitrary system commands by injecting shell metacharacters.

bug_commits.author: Alexei Ledenev (aider)
bug_commits.message: refactor: Enhance AWS MCP server with rate limiting, auth error detection, and command validation

llm_verdict.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced the `validate_aws_command` function, which relies on `shlex.split` for validation but does not prevent shell injection when the command is subsequently executed via `asyncio.create_subprocess_shell` (or similar shell-based executors). By implementing the validation logic in this commit, it established the flawed security boundary that failed to account for shell metacharacters being passed to the underlying shell execution.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: The server failed to properly sanitize or validate user-provided input before passing it to a shell-based command executor, allowing attackers to execute arbitrary system commands by injecting shell metacharacters.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: subprocess.run(f"aws {user_input}", shell=True)
llm_verdict.causal_chain: The commit introduced a validation function that used shlex.split to parse commands, but the application continued to use shell-based execution (subprocess_shell) for the validated strings. Because the validation logic did not strictly sanitize or escape the input for shell context, it allowed attackers to bypass the check using shell metacharacters, leading to command injection.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The command-execution weakness existed before 23c717b6: the initial commit cbe3319 already validated only that the input started with `aws` and then passed the full string to `asyncio.create_subprocess_shell(command)`, which is the core injection sink. Commit 23c717b6 introduced `validate_aws_command()` and a small denylist, but kept the same shell-based execution path, so it added an incomplete mitigation rather than introducing the vulnerability. For the piped-command path, the removed `validate_pipe_command()` logic in the fix traces to later commit 3f2b8c55, and the actual shell-based pipe execution was also introduced there in `tools.py`, not by 23c717b6.
evidence: Initial commit cbe3319821dfa54fdd8b1b1811e8a597cc639cbc already had `cmd_parts = shlex.split(command)` + only `aws` prefix checking, followed by `asyncio.create_subprocess_shell(command, ...)` in `src/aws_mcp_server/utils/cli_executor.py`.

Diff for 23c717b627ea5b0e0e71420c360bf0dfb85f4365 shows it added `validate_aws_command()` and replaced the inline prefix check with a call to that helper, but did not change the `create_subprocess_shell(command)` sink.

Blame on pre-fix `src/aws_mcp_server/cli_executor.py` at commit 2f4271705728eae7d3f3cbee23a8c355487cdacb attributes `validate_aws_command()` to 23c717b6, but `validate_pipe_command()` to 3f2b8c55727eef3275577ad71733cc314ed63143, showing the fix removed logic from multiple introducers.

Commit 3f2b8c55727eef3275577ad71733cc314ed63143 added `src/aws_mcp_server/tools.py` with `execute_piped_command()` using `asyncio.create_subprocess_shell(pipe_command, ...)`, and blame on those lines points to 3f2b8c55, not 23c717b6.

Later commits 2f4271705728eae7d3f3cbee23a8c355487cdacb and 077c67fdf533d261df4f5b63e6cc7291a5025965 explicitly replaced `create_subprocess_shell` with `create_subprocess_exec` for regular and piped commands, indicating the shell execution issue predated the suspected BIC.

bug_commits.message: refactor: Replace create_subprocess_shell with create_subprocess_exec for better security

The vulnerability likely existed in a different part of the codebase or was introduced by a subsequent change that reverted or bypassed these safety measures.

cves.description: Titra APIs have Improper Access Control
cves.how_introduced: The blamed commit introduced the pattern of merging user-controlled JSON input directly into object literals (e.g., passing 'json.customfields' to 'insertTimeCard' and other database operations) across multiple newly defined API routes, thereby enabling mass assignment by allowing user input to overwrite internal fields.
cves.root_cause: The application spreads user-controlled `customfields` at the end of object literals, allowing attackers to overwrite explicitly defined sensitive fields like `projectId` or `userId`.

cves.description: OpenClaw's unsanitized session ID enables path traversal in transcript file operations
cves.how_introduced: The blamed commit modified `resolveSessionTranscriptPath` to dynamically construct a filename using the `sessionId` parameter and then passed it directly to `path.join` without any validation or sanitization, allowing directory traversal sequences in the `sessionId` to escape the intended directory.
cves.root_cause: The application failed to sanitize user-provided session IDs before using them to construct file system paths, allowing attackers to escape the intended session directory via directory traversal sequences (e.g., ../).

bug_commits.author: hsrvc
bug_commits.message: Implement Phase 2: Topic-level message history isolation for multi-topic Telegram support

llm_verdict.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced the vulnerable pattern `path.join(resolveAgentSessionsDir(agentId), fileName)` where `fileName` is constructed directly from the unsanitized `sessionId`. This change replaced a simpler path construction with one that explicitly incorporates the user-provided `sessionId` into the filename, creating the path traversal vector addressed in the fix.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: The application failed to sanitize user-provided session IDs before using them to construct file system paths, allowing attackers to escape the intended session directory via directory traversal sequences (e.g., ../).
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: path.join(baseDir, sessionId, filename)
llm_verdict.causal_chain: The blamed commit modified `resolveSessionTranscriptPath` to dynamically construct a filename using the `sessionId` parameter and then passed it directly to `path.join` without any validation or sanitization, allowing directory traversal sequences in the `sessionId` to escape the intended directory.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The fix hardens two preexisting unsafe patterns: `resolveSessionTranscriptPath` now validates `sessionId`, and `resolveSessionFilePath` now constrains `entry.sessionFile` to stay under the sessions directory. Blame on the pre-fix `src/config/sessions/paths.ts` attributes the transcript-path lines to the Jan 14 split/refactor (`bcbfb357`) and Jan 15 topic-id adjustment (`725a6b71`), while the `sessionFile` passthrough traces to `7e5cef29`, not to `8da4f259`. More importantly, the unsanitized `sessionId` path join already existed before `8da4f259`: at `5400766` the code was already `return path.join(resolveAgentSessionsDir(agentId), `${sessionId}.jsonl`)`, so the suspected commit only extended the existing logic with `topicId` rather than introducing the traversal flaw.
evidence: Fix commit `4199f988` adds `validateSessionId()` and `resolvePathWithinSessionsDir()`, replacing raw `path.join(..., `${sessionId}.jsonl`)` and raw `candidate ? candidate : ...` behavior in `src/config/sessions/paths.ts`.

Blame at pre-fix state (`db137dd65`) for `src/config/sessions/paths.ts` lines 37-60 shows `resolveSessionTranscriptPath` came from `bcbfb357`/`725a6b71`, and the vulnerable `resolveSessionFilePath` return path traces back to `c379191f`/earlier `7e5cef29`, not `8da4f259`.

`8da4f259`'s diff on `src/config/sessions.ts` only adds optional `topicId` handling: it changes `return path.join(..., `${sessionId}.jsonl`)` into a `fileName` ternary plus `path.join(..., fileName)`.

At the previous file revision `5400766`, before `8da4f259`, `resolveSessionTranscriptPath` already contained the unsanitized `sessionId` join: `return path.join(resolveAgentSessionsDir(agentId), `${sessionId}.jsonl`)`.

History goes back even further: at `aa45f512` / blamed to `fece42ce`, `resolveSessionTranscriptPath(sessionId)` already returned `path.join(resolveSessionTranscriptsDir(), `${sessionId}.jsonl`)`, confirming the flaw predates the suspected commit.

cves.description: mcp-memory-service: System Information Disclosure via Health Endpoint

Before 0516cecc, `health.py` already exposed `/health` version/timestamp/uptime and `/health/detailed` system fingerprinting data plus `database_path` with no auth at all; 0516cecc only added a conditional `require_read_access` dependency to `/health/detailed`, which was a partial security improvement. Blame on the pre-fix file shows the leaked `/health` fields came from earlier commits, the leaked `database_path` lines came from 956a32c23e, and even the exact `require_read_access` line later changed to `require_write_access` was last touched by 3a922924, not 0516cecc.

`get_commit_diff` for `0516cecc0b29a29e9fc03c9821a6f578c0dad282` shows only a conditional OAuth import plus `user: AuthenticationResult = Depends(require_read_access) if OAUTH_ENABLED else None` added to `detailed_health_check`; it did not add the leaked fields or modify `/health`.

`blame_lines` on pre-fix `src/mcp_memory_service/web/api/health.py` shows `/health` response lines 71-75 blamed to older commits (`4e796e2814`, `dda7311bfc`, `8aa4b9134e`), system-info lines 90-100 blamed to `4e796e2814`, and `database_path` lines 131-132 blamed to `956a32c23e`.

cves.description: zeptoclaw has Shell allowlist-blocklist bypass via command/argument injection and file name wildcards
cves.how_introduced: The blamed commit added a blocklist for encoded/indirect execution using rigid regexes that expected exact flag sequences. This allowed attackers to bypass the blocklist by simply inserting extra flags (like `python -Bc`) or using shell globbing, leading to the vulnerability.
cves.root_cause: The shell command blocklist used overly strict regexes that could be bypassed by inserting additional flags (e.g., `python -Bc`), and literal string matching that could be bypassed using shell globbing characters (e.g., `/etc/pass[w]d`).

bug_commits.author: qhkmdev90
bug_commits.message: security: fix 6 findings from code security review + pre-launch prep

llm_verdict.reasoning: The blamed commit directly introduced the overly strict regex patterns (e.g., `r"python[23]?\s+-c\s+"`) that were identified as vulnerable to bypasses using additional flags. The fix commit specifically modifies these exact lines to be more permissive of intervening flags.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: The shell command blocklist used overly strict regexes that could be bypassed by inserting additional flags (e.g., `python -Bc`), and literal string matching that could be bypassed using shell globbing characters (e.g., `/etc/pass[w]d`).
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: r"python[23]?\s+-c\s+" and literal string matching without resolving shell globs.
llm_verdict.causal_chain: The blamed commit added a blocklist for encoded/indirect execution using rigid regexes that expected exact flag sequences. This allowed attackers to bypass the blocklist by simply inserting extra flags (like `python -Bc`) or using shell globbing, leading to the vulnerability.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The suspected commit d3480ca9 did author the weak inline-code regexes (`python[23]?\s+-c\s+`, `perl\s+-e\s+`, etc.) that the fix later broadened, but its parent ebd4d9e8 had no such checks at all: d348 was adding a new defense-in-depth blocklist, not creating the ability to execute those commands. The other GHSA-5wp8-q9mx-8jx8 flaws fixed here were introduced elsewhere: the glob/literal bypass already existed before d348 via simple `command_lower.contains(literal)` matching, and the allowlist chaining / empty-allowlist bugs were added later by 1712debb when allowlisting was introduced. Per the 'insufficient mitigation' rule, d348 is related hardening code, not the true bug-introducing commit for this vulnerability.
evidence: Blame on the pre-fix file (1b471c6) shows lines 130-138—the old `python[23]?\s+-c\s+`, `perl\s+-e\s+`, `ruby\s+-e\s+`, `node\s+-e\s+` patterns—were introduced by d3480ca9.

The file at d3480ca9's parent (ebd4d9e8) contains none of those scripting-language blocklist patterns, and d348's own message says 'Shell blocklist — add 11 bypass patterns', confirming it was a security-hardening addition rather than the origin of the exposure.
The file at ebd4d9e8 already used `for literal in &self.literal_patterns { if command_lower.contains(literal) { ... } }`, so the wildcard/glob bypass fixed by de-globbing existed before d348.

Blame on the pre-fix allowlist logic (lines 317-344 in 1b471c6) attributes the first-token-only check and `&& !self.allowlist.is_empty()` guard to 1712debbea60..., meaning the allowlist command-chaining and empty-allowlist bugs were introduced later, not by d348.

cves.description: mcp-memory-service Vulnerable to System Information Disclosure via Health Endpoint
cves.how_introduced: The blamed commit added `require_read_access` to the detailed health check, which was insufficient to protect sensitive system metadata, thereby exposing it to unauthorized users.
cves.root_cause: The health endpoint exposed sensitive system information such as version, uptime, and database paths to unauthenticated or read-only users.

bug_commits.author: Henry
bug_commits.message: feat: OAuth 2.1 Dynamic Client Registration for Claude Code HTTP Transport (#117)

llm_verdict.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced the `require_read_access` dependency to the `/health/detailed` endpoint, which allowed any authenticated user (including those with only read-only access) to view the sensitive system information. The fix commit explicitly identifies this as the source of the information disclosure and restricts access to `require_write_access` to mitigate the vulnerability.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: The health endpoint exposed sensitive system information such as version, uptime, and database paths to unauthenticated or read-only users.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: Returning detailed system information (e.g., version, uptime) in a public or read-only health check endpoint.
llm_verdict.causal_chain: The blamed commit added `require_read_access` to the detailed health check, which was insufficient to protect sensitive system metadata, thereby exposing it to unauthorized users.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The fix hardens several disclosure paths in `health.py`: it strips version/timestamp/uptime from public `/health`, removes OS/Python/hardware and `database_path` data from `/health/detailed`, and raises some endpoints from read access to write access. But `0516cecc` did not introduce those disclosures; the same `/health` fields and the same detailed system/storage leakage were already present in the immediately prior file revision (`a9ccb97...`). In fact, `0516cecc` only added a conditional `require_read_access` dependency to `/health/detailed`, which is a partial security improvement over the previously unauthenticated endpoint, so this matches an insufficient-mitigation case rather than bug introduction.
evidence: `get_file_at_commit(a9ccb97..., health.py)` shows that before `0516cecc`, `/health` already returned `version`, `timestamp`, and `uptime_seconds`, and `/health/detailed` already exposed `platform`, `platform_version`, `python_version`, memory/disk sizes, and `database_path` with no auth dependency.

`get_commit_diff(0516cecc, health.py)` shows the suspected BIC only imported `OAUTH_ENABLED`/`require_read_access` and changed `detailed_health_check` to `user: AuthenticationResult = Depends(require_read_access) if OAUTH_ENABLED else None`; it did not add the sensitive response fields.

Blame on the pre-fix file state (`blame_lines` at `06f4ef40...`) attributes the public `/health` disclosure lines to earlier commits (`4e796e2814` and `dda7311bfc`), the detailed `system_info` fingerprinting lines to `4e796e2814`, and `database_path` to `956a32c23e`, not to `0516cecc`.

The auth line that the fix strengthens to `require_write_access` was later normalized by `3a92292477` from the BIC’s conditional read-access form to unconditional read access, reinforcing that `0516cecc` added a mitigation rather than creating the vulnerable disclosure behavior.

bug_commits.author: doobidoo
bug_commits.message: fix: resolve datetime deprecation warnings and improve mobile UX (v5.0.5)

llm_verdict.reasoning: The blamed commit only refactored the timestamp generation to use timezone-aware datetime objects, which is a standard best practice. It did not introduce the vulnerable logic (exposing system info), as that code was already present in the file prior to this commit.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: The health endpoint exposed sensitive system information such as version, uptime, and database paths to unauthenticated or read-only users.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: Returning detailed system information (e.g., version, uptime) in a public or read-only health check endpoint.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The fix hardens several disclosure points in `health.py`: it removes `version`/`timestamp`/`uptime_seconds` from the public `/health` response, strips sensitive system/storage details from `/health/detailed`, and upgrades `/health/detailed`, `/memory-stats`, and `/clear-caches` from read access to write access. Blame on the pre-fix file shows those security-relevant lines mostly came from other commits: the original public health and detailed disclosure logic was created in `4e796e2814`, `version` was added in `dda7311bfc`, `database_path` was added in `956a32c23e`, and the read-access guards were added later in `3a92292477`/`0516cecc0b`. The suspected commit `8aa4b9134e` only replaced `datetime.utcnow()` with `datetime.now(timezone.utc)` on two timestamp lines; the immediately previous file revision already exposed the same data, so this is blame drift from a trivial edit rather than true vulnerability introduction.
evidence: `8aa4b9134ef8b2c59954dd1afa8760bfccfc31e1` diff for `src/mcp_memory_service/web/api/health.py` changes only two timestamp expressions plus the `timezone` import; it does not add any new endpoint, auth behavior, or disclosed fields.

Pre-BIC file state at `cb5e5b02b96eb4ff47c4ca59063077f709f7b771` already had `/health` returning `version`, `timestamp`, and `uptime_seconds`, and `/health/detailed` exposing platform/Python/memory/disk details plus `database_path` with no auth parameter at all.

Blame at pre-fix state (`06f4ef40bc7a799e929fda623c360a03185bb823`) attributes the sensitive system disclosure lines 90-100 to `4e796e2814`, `database_path` line 132 to `956a32c23e`, and the `require_read_access` dependency lines for detailed/memory endpoints to `3a92292477`; only the timestamp lines 74 and 186 blame to `8aa4b9134e`.

`4e796e2814149f966901dc59528da230a9da93b3` created `health.py` with public `/health` timestamp/uptime and unauthenticated `/health/detailed` system/storage disclosure; `dda7311bfca04e994fddc78fb4561edae32e8413` later added the `version` field, showing the vulnerable pattern predates `8aa4b9134e`.

bug_commits.author: Henry
bug_commits.message: fix: Remove OAuth conditional from all API route dependencies

llm_verdict.reasoning: The blamed commit merely removes the 'if OAUTH_ENABLED else None' condition from the authentication dependency, enforcing 'require_read_access' unconditionally. It did not introduce the sensitive system information fields nor the original use of read-only access for the detailed health endpoint, meaning the vulnerability existed prior to this commit.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: The health endpoint exposed sensitive system information such as version, uptime, and database paths to unauthenticated or read-only users.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: Returning detailed system information (e.g., version, uptime) in a public or read-only health check endpoint.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The core information-disclosure behavior fixed in GHSA-73hc-m4hx-79pj predated 3a922924: before that commit, /health already returned version/timestamp/uptime, and /health/detailed already exposed OS/Python/CPU/memory/disk details plus database_path. Blame on the pre-fix file attributes those leaked fields to earlier commits (notably 4e796e2814149f966901dc59528da230a9da93b3, dda7311bfca04e994fddc78fb4561edae32e8413, 8aa4b9134ef8b2c59954dd1afa8760bfccfc31e1, and 956a32c23e2fb0014794913145b62908bb0fddd6). Commit 3a922924 only changed the auth dependencies from conditional read access / no auth when OAuth was disabled to unconditional require_read_access, which was a security improvement later strengthened to require_write_access.
evidence: In 69ea9074d1c91ed454c5f9ab2f15eb81bee6f1f0 (the file state before 3a922924), health.py already had /health returning status+version+timestamp+uptime and /health/detailed exposing platform, platform_version, python_version, cpu_count, memory/disk totals, and storage_info['database_path'].

The diff for 3a9229247766246d8bfb3be3ca2d4f0954ea76d6 shows only auth-plumbing changes in health.py: removing OAUTH_ENABLED conditionals and making Depends(require_read_access) unconditional; it did not add any of the sensitive response fields later removed by the fix.

Blame on the pre-fix file (06f4ef40bc7a799e929fda623c360a03185bb823) attributes /health response lines to earlier commits: 4e796e2814149f966901dc59528da230a9da93b3 created timestamp/uptime and the endpoint, dda7311bfca04e994fddc78fb4561edae32e8413 added version, and 8aa4b9134ef8b2c59954dd1afa8760bfccfc31e1 updated the timestamp call.

The same blame run attributes detailed system_info fields to 4e796e2814149f966901dc59528da230a9da93b3 and database_path exposure to 956a32c23e2fb0014794913145b62908bb0fddd6; only the read-access dependency line is blamed to 3a922924.

Commit 0516cecc0b29a29e9fc03c9821a6f578c0dad282 had already added protection to /health/detailed as Depends(require_read_access) if OAUTH_ENABLED else None; 3a922924 merely removed the 'if OAUTH_ENABLED else None' fallback, i.e. strengthened access control rather than introducing the disclosure.

cves.description: OpenClaw: Feishu reaction events could bypass group authorization and mention gating
cves.how_introduced: The initial implementation in the blamed commit failed to map the `chat_type` field from the Feishu API response into the `FeishuMessageInfo` structure. Without this field, subsequent security logic downstream could not determine whether an event originated from a authorized group or an unauthorized private chat, allowing the bypass.
cves.root_cause: The application failed to preserve or validate the chat type context during Feishu reaction events, allowing users to bypass group authorization and mention gating mechanisms.

bug_commits.author: Yifeng Wang
bug_commits.message: feat(feishu): replace built-in SDK with community plugin

llm_verdict.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced the `FeishuMessageInfo` type and the `getMessageFeishu` function without including `chat_type` validation or handling. This architectural omission meant that downstream logic processing Feishu events lacked the necessary chat context to perform authorization or mention gating checks, directly leading to the described vulnerability.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: The application failed to preserve or validate the chat type context during Feishu reaction events, allowing users to bypass group authorization and mention gating mechanisms.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: Missing or improper verification of chat authorization context (chat type) when processing incoming reaction events from Feishu.
llm_verdict.causal_chain: The initial implementation in the blamed commit failed to map the `chat_type` field from the Feishu API response into the `FeishuMessageInfo` structure. Without this field, subsequent security logic downstream could not determine whether an event originated from a authorized group or an unauthorized private chat, allowing the bypass.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: Commit 2267d58 did create the new `extensions/feishu/src/send.ts` helper and the pre-fix `getMessageFeishu()` lines that omitted `chatType`, but at that point the codebase had no Feishu reaction-event ingestion path at all. The actual bypass was introduced later when reaction support was added in `monitor.ts`/`monitor.account.ts`: those later commits synthesized reaction messages with `chat_id` from the looked-up message but defaulted `chat_type` to `p2p`, which bypassed group authorization and mention checks. So 2267d58 is related background code, but it did not itself introduce the vulnerable behavior described by GHSA-m69h-jm2f-2pv8.
evidence: At fix-1 state (`63ce7c74...`), blame for `send.ts` shows the returned `chatId`/missing `chatType` structure came from `2267d58`, but blame for the security-critical reaction synthesis in `monitor.account.ts` points to later commits `f46bd2e0...` and `3043e68d...` (`syntheticChatType = event.chat_type === "group" ? "group" : "p2p"`).

`2267d58` added `extensions/feishu/src/send.ts` and `getMessageFeishu()`, but repository search at that commit shows it was only used from `bot.ts` for quoted/replied message lookup; there is no `im.message.reaction.created_v1` handler in that commit.

The reaction-event path first appears later in commit `8241145ada40` (`feat(feishu): add reaction event support`), which imports `getMessageFeishu` into `monitor.ts`, adds `resolveReactionSyntheticEvent`, and defaults `syntheticChatType` to `event.chat_type ?? "p2p"`.

The fix commit hardens `resolveReactionSyntheticEvent` to preserve/normalize looked-up chat type and fail closed when chat type cannot be resolved; that makes `monitor.account.ts` the core security fix, while the `send.ts` `chatType` addition is a supporting data-propagation change.

While it defined a `chatType`, the fix commit reveals that the system lacked a more comprehensive `FeishuMessageInfo` structure and an expanded `FeishuChatType` (including 'private') to properly validate context during reaction events, which led to the authorization bypass.

cves.description: Fickling has Static Analysis Bypass via Incomplete Dangerous Module Blocklist
cves.how_introduced: The blamed commit added `extract_identifier_from_ast_node` which recursively processes `ast.Call` nodes to extract identifiers. This implementation did not enforce strict validation on the resulting module strings, allowing attackers to craft pickle payloads that resolve to dangerous modules (like cProfile) which were not correctly caught by the existing blocklist logic.
cves.root_cause: The application's blocklist for unsafe pickle deserialization could be bypassed by using the cProfile.run() function, allowing arbitrary code execution.

bug_commits.author: Lucas Bourtoule
bug_commits.message: Fix ast.unparse() crash with malformed pickle files

llm_verdict.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced the logic for extracting identifiers from AST nodes, including the handling of ast.Call nodes. By failing to properly validate or restrict the module/attribute strings extracted from these nodes, it created the mechanism that allowed bypasses like cProfile.run() to evade the blocklist.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: The application's blocklist for unsafe pickle deserialization could be bypassed by using the cProfile.run() function, allowing arbitrary code execution.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: cProfile.run()
llm_verdict.causal_chain: The blamed commit added `extract_identifier_from_ast_node` which recursively processes `ast.Call` nodes to extract identifiers. This implementation did not enforce strict validation on the resulting module strings, allowing attackers to craft pickle payloads that resolve to dangerous modules (like cProfile) which were not correctly caught by the existing blocklist logic.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The core vulnerable pattern fixed by GHSA-q5qq-mvfm-j35x is the exact-match dangerous-module check in `Pickled.unsafe_imports()` (`if node.module in (...)`), and blame on the pre-fix file attributes that line to much older commits, not to 5e054ddc. At `5e054ddc^`, `unsafe_imports()` already used the same exact-match logic, so the incomplete blocklist existed before the suspected BIC. Commit 5e054ddc only changed `StackGlobal.run` to extract and validate identifiers; the fix's tweak from `module.isidentifier()` to `all(m.isidentifier() for m in module.split('.'))` is a follow-up adjustment to that crash-handling logic, not the introduction of the blocklist bypass itself.
evidence: Blame at pre-fix commit `b793563e60a5e039c5837b09d7f4f6b92e6040d1` shows `fickling/fickle.py:867` (`if node.module in (` in `unsafe_imports`) attributed to old commit `670b280e`, not `5e054ddc`.

`fickling/fickle.py` at `5e054ddc^` (`f018ac1a`) already contains the exact vulnerable `unsafe_imports()` pattern: `if node.module in (...)`, proving the incomplete root-module check predates the suspected BIC.
The diff for `5e054ddc` only modifies `StackGlobal.run` and adds `extract_identifier_from_ast_node()`; it does not touch `unsafe_imports()` or the dangerous-module blocklist.

Blame on pre-fix `fickling/fickle.py:1160-1164` shows the `module.isidentifier()` validation line was introduced by `5e054ddc`, but the fix changes it only to allow dotted module names (`module.split('.')`), i.e. a refinement of BIC's malformed-pickle handling rather than the original static-analysis bypass.

cves.description: Taylor has race condition in /get-patch that allows purchase token replay
cves.how_introduced: The commit introduced a pattern where the application first queried the database to validate a token (SELECT) and subsequently performed a separate update (UPDATE) to mark it as used, creating a window for concurrent requests to bypass the validation.
cves.root_cause: The application performed a check-then-act operation by querying the database for a token's status and subsequently updating it in a separate transaction, allowing multiple concurrent requests to use the same token before it was marked as used.

llm_verdict.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced the initial implementation of the /get-patch endpoint, which contained the vulnerable check-then-act logic. By implementing the database query and update as two separate, non-atomic operations, it established the race condition that the fix commit later addressed.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: The application performed a check-then-act operation by querying the database for a token's status and subsequently updating it in a separate transaction, allowing multiple concurrent requests to use the same token before it was marked as used.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: db.get(SELECT...) followed by db.run(UPDATE...) without atomicity
llm_verdict.causal_chain: The commit introduced a pattern where the application first queried the database to validate a token (SELECT) and subsequently performed a separate update (UPDATE) to mark it as used, creating a window for concurrent requests to bypass the validation.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The fix specifically removes a non-atomic check-then-update sequence in `/get-patch` and replaces it with a single atomic `UPDATE ... AND token_used_at IS NULL`, so the vulnerable pattern is the later replay-prevention logic, not the original endpoint creation. Blame on the pre-fix file attributes the relevant `/get-patch` lines to 5e5a80b5 and the `token_used_at` schema line to 57b76343, not to c139c021. In c139c021, `/get-patch` had no `token_used_at` field or consume-on-use logic at all; that race only appeared when later commits tried to enforce one-time token use with a separate `SELECT` followed by `UPDATE`.
evidence: `fdf67a6` fixes `/get-patch` by replacing `db.get(... token_used_at ...)` plus later `db.run(UPDATE purchases SET token_used_at...)` with one atomic `db.run` conditioned on `token_used_at IS NULL`.

`blame_lines` on `45b69f6` (the state before the fix) shows lines 297-317 in `/get-patch` blamed to `5e5a80b5`, while the `token_used_at` schema line 119 is blamed to `57b76343`; the suspected BIC `c139c021` does not own the vulnerable logic.

`git_log` for `templates/backend-in-a-box/index.js` shows the sequence `c139c021` (file added) -> `57b76343` ('Enhance Taysell security and usability') -> `5e5a80b5` -> `45b69f6` -> fix, indicating the replay-protection code was introduced after the initial file creation.

At `c139c021`, `templates/backend-in-a-box/index.js` contains `/get-patch` with only `SELECT * FROM purchases ... status = 'COMPLETED'` and patch return; repository search at that commit finds no `token_used_at` anywhere, confirming the specific race-fixed code did not yet exist.

bug_commits.author: google-labs-jules[bot]
bug_commits.message: Enhance Taysell security and usability

llm_verdict.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced the `token_used_at` column to the `purchases` table and implemented the logic to track token usage. Crucially, it established the vulnerable 'check-then-act' pattern where the application first checks if a token is used and then updates it in a separate step, which is the root cause of the race condition fixed in the subsequent commit.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: The application performed a check-then-act operation by querying the database for a token's status and subsequently updating it in a separate transaction, allowing multiple concurrent requests to use the same token before it was marked as used.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: db.get(SELECT...) followed by db.run(UPDATE...) without atomicity
llm_verdict.causal_chain: 1. The blamed commit adds the 'token_used_at' column to the database schema. 2. It introduces the logic to verify if a token has been used before providing the patch. 3. By implementing this as a non-atomic sequence (SELECT then UPDATE), it created the race condition window allowing token replay.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The fix hardens `/get-patch` by replacing a non-atomic `SELECT`/check/`UPDATE` sequence with a single atomic `UPDATE ... WHERE token_used_at IS NULL` before serving the patch. Blame on the vulnerable pre-fix lines in `45b69f6` points to `5e5a80b5` for the actual `db.get(...)`, `row.token_used_at` check, and post-read `db.run(...)` logic, not to `57b76343`. More importantly, the file at `c139c021` (before the suspected BIC) already allowed replay outright with no `token_used_at` check at all; `57b76343` introduced a replay-prevention mechanism (`token_used_at`) that was later strengthened, so it is better classified as an insufficient mitigation rather than the commit that introduced the vulnerability.
evidence: Fix `fdf67a6` changes `/get-patch` from `SELECT` then later `UPDATE token_used_at` to a single atomic `UPDATE ... AND token_used_at IS NULL` before sending the patch.
`blame_lines` on `45b69f6` for `/get-patch` shows lines 297-302 and 310-317 are attributed to `5e5a80b5`, while `57b76343` only owns a blank line in that region.
Pre-BIC file `c139c021` has `/get-patch` with `SELECT * FROM purchases ...` followed directly by reading/sending the patch; `token_used_at` does not exist there, so replay existed before `57b76343`.
Suspected BIC `57b76343` explicitly added `token_used_at` and checks/updates it as 'Protection Against Replay Attacks', indicating it added a security control that the later fix made atomic.

cves.description: OpenClaw affected by SSRF via attachment/media URL hydration
cves.how_introduced:
1. The blamed commit adds the 'OpenResponses' HTTP API and media processing capabilities (image/PDF tool support).
2. This introduced code paths that fetch external content based on user-provided input in the gateway.
3. The logic lacked validation against internal network addresses, creating the SSRF vulnerability fixed by the subsequent implementation of the SSRF policy enforcement in `src/infra/net/ssrf.ts`.
cves.root_cause: The application allowed remote media fetches without validating destination URLs, enabling attackers to make arbitrary requests to internal network resources.

bug_commits.author: Peter Steinberger
bug_commits.message: fix: expand /v1/responses inputs (#1229) (thanks @RyanLisse)

llm_verdict.reasoning: The blamed commit modifies the 'src/gateway/openresponses-http.ts' file to implement API handling and PDF/Canvas processing, but it does not contain the vulnerable 'fetch' logic or the 'web-fetch.ts' file identified in the fix. The vulnerability is located in the agent tools for remote fetching, whereas this commit focuses on the gateway's HTTP interface and response formatting.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: The application performed remote URL fetching without adequate validation of the destination IP addresses or hostnames, allowing attackers to probe internal network resources.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: fetch(url, { redirect: 'manual', ... }) without validating the resolved IP address against a blocklist of internal/private network ranges

agent_verdicts.reasoning: 81c68f58 fixes SSRF by routing remote-media fetches through a shared guarded fetch helper, with the key security change in `src/media/fetch.ts` replacing `res = await fetcher(url)` and in callers like `src/web/media.ts` / `src/slack/monitor/media.ts`. Blame on the pre-fix vulnerable lines points to earlier commits (`4075895c` for `src/media/fetch.ts`, `5bd55037`/`b623557a` for `src/agents/tools/web-fetch.ts`), not to `bbc67f37`, and `bbc67f37` does not touch any of the files patched by 81c68. Although `bbc67f37` did expand `/v1/responses` input handling, it added its own SSRF checks there and that code was later refactored into `src/media/input-files.ts`; that is adjacent feature work / blame drift, not the vulnerable raw media fetch that 81c68 actually fixes.
evidence: `blame_lines` on `src/media/fetch.ts` at the fix parent shows `res = await fetcher(url)` was introduced by `4075895c4ca491753cd13eeffbb833f091514daa` on 2026-01-10, well before `bbc67f37`.

`get_commit_info` for `bbc67f375472da427cdb831b6301f077bad97a30` shows it changed gateway/OpenResponses files only; it did not modify `src/media/fetch.ts`, `src/web/media.ts`, `src/slack/monitor/media.ts`, `src/media/input-files.ts`, or `src/agents/tools/web-fetch.ts`.

cves.description: OpenClaw: stageSandboxMedia destination symlink traversal can overwrite files outside sandbox workspace
cves.how_introduced: The blamed commit added the `dest` path calculation using `path.join(destDir, fileName)` and the subsequent `fs.copyFile(source, dest)` call, which lacks symlink-aware boundary checks, allowing for arbitrary file overwrites.
cves.root_cause: The stageSandboxMedia function failed to validate that the destination path within the sandbox was not a symlink pointing outside the workspace, allowing attackers to overwrite arbitrary files on the host system.

bug_commits.author: Tyler Yust
bug_commits.message: iMessage: Add remote attachment support for VM/SSH deployments

llm_verdict.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced the logic that calculates the destination path (`dest`) and performs the file copy operation using `fs.copyFile(source, dest)` without any validation to ensure `dest` is not a symlink. This directly matches the vulnerable pattern described, where files can be overwritten outside the intended directory if a symlink exists at the destination path.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: The stageSandboxMedia function failed to validate that the destination path within the sandbox was not a symlink pointing outside the workspace, allowing attackers to overwrite arbitrary files on the host system.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: fs.copyFileSync(source, destinationPath) or similar direct file operations without root-scoped boundary validation
llm_verdict.causal_chain: The blamed commit added the `dest` path calculation using `path.join(destDir, fileName)` and the subsequent `fs.copyFile(source, dest)` call, which lacks symlink-aware boundary checks, allowing for arbitrary file overwrites.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The security fix hardens destination writes in `stageSandboxMedia` by replacing direct `fs.copyFile`/`scp` writes to `path.join(..., fileName)` with `writeFileWithinRoot`, specifically to stop destination symlink traversal. But the last pre-BIC version of this file (`c379191f80f`, the parent of the suspected commit) already wrote directly to `<sandbox>/media/inbound/<file>` via `fs.copyFile`, so the core sandbox overwrite primitive existed before `7a9ff182`. The suspected commit mainly added remote-attachment support and wrapped the existing local copy in a remote/local conditional; for the sandbox-local path that the fix test exercises, semantics were unchanged.
evidence: Before the suspected commit, `src/auto-reply/reply/stage-sandbox-media.ts` at `c379191f80f` already had: `const destDir = path.join(sandbox.workspaceDir, "media", "inbound"); ... const dest = path.join(destDir, fileName); await fs.copyFile(source, dest);`.

Blame on the pre-fix file shows the core destination-path construction predates the BIC: line 142 (`const dest = path.join(destDir, fileName);`) is blamed to `ea018a68ccb`, while only the later remote-support conditional around copy/scp is blamed to `7a9ff182`.

The fix-added regression test is `blocks destination symlink escapes when staging into sandbox workspace`, and it reproduces a local sandbox symlink escape (symlinking `sandboxDir/media` to an outside directory), matching the pre-BIC local `fs.copyFile` behavior rather than a new remote-only path.

The suspected commit’s diff preserves sandbox behavior when a sandbox exists: old `path.join(sandbox.workspaceDir, "media", "inbound")` became `sandbox ? path.join(effectiveWorkspaceDir, "media", "inbound") : effectiveWorkspaceDir`, with `effectiveWorkspaceDir = sandbox?.workspaceDir ?? remoteMediaCacheDir`, so the sandbox-local vulnerable write path was not newly introduced there.

cves.description: OpenEMR allows inconsistent escaping of translation function output
cves.how_introduced: The blamed commit refactored the echo statement to remove the xl() function's padding arguments, which inadvertently exposed the raw $temp_url variable to the output stream without the necessary text() sanitization wrapper.
cves.root_cause: The application fails to properly sanitize user-supplied input before rendering it in the HTML output, allowing an attacker to inject malicious scripts.

bug_commits.author: Copilot
bug_commits.message: refactor: remove deprecated mode, prepend, append parameters from xl() (#9960)

llm_verdict.reasoning: The blamed commit modified the line in question by removing extra arguments from the xl() function call, which directly resulted in the raw $temp_url variable being concatenated to the output without any sanitization. This change established the vulnerable pattern where user-controlled or filesystem-derived paths are echoed directly to the browser.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: The application fails to properly sanitize user-supplied input before rendering it in the HTML output, allowing an attacker to inject malicious scripts.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: echo (!empty($vis_date)) ? $vis_date : date('Y-m-d');
llm_verdict.causal_chain: The blamed commit refactored the echo statement to remove the xl() function's padding arguments, which inadvertently exposed the raw $temp_url variable to the output stream without the necessary text() sanitization wrapper.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The fix in `controllers/C_Document.class.php` addresses an unescaped HTML output pattern by replacing `xl(...)` with `xlt(...)` and wrapping `$temp_url` with `text()`. Although blame on the pre-fix line points to `cce6e7139a25b6508f992ba727dc2fba5872019d`, history shows that before that commit the same code already emitted unescaped translation output and the raw path: `echo xl(..., '', '', ' ') . $temp_url;`. Commit `cce6e713` only refactored the callsite to remove deprecated `xl()` parameters (`xl(...) . ' ' . $temp_url`) without changing the escaping behavior, so this is blame drift from a trivial refactor rather than the true introduction of the vulnerability.
evidence: At the state before the fix (`1057b54274f65312914883b137c2a2068be61b66`), `blame_lines` attributes line 419 to `cce6e7139a25b6508f992ba727dc2fba5872019d`, confirming why it was suspected.

`get_file_at_commit` for `994f13eb4b24741bb71cb73486084351519d9575` (before `cce6e713`) shows the same vulnerable pattern already present: `echo xl('The requested document ...', '', '', ' ') . $temp_url;`.
`blame_lines` on `994f13eb4b24741bb71cb73486084351519d9575` attributes that pre-BIC vulnerable line to older commit `f8c1be2bad65b0641cfd4b17a74f835cc5ffc9ae` (2022-01-04), not to `cce6e713`.

The diff for `cce6e7139a25b6508f992ba727dc2fba5872019d` shows only a syntactic refactor of this line from `xl(..., '', '', ' ') . $temp_url` to `xl(...) . ' ' . $temp_url`; escaping remained unchanged.

`library/translation.inc.php` before and after `cce6e713` shows `xl()` returns untranslated/unescaped text, while `library/htmlspecialchars.inc.php` already provided `xlt()` as the escaped wrapper before `cce6e713`, confirming the later fix was switching to an existing safe API rather than correcting behavior introduced by `cce6e713`.

cves.description: Fickling has Static Analysis Bypass via Incomplete Dangerous Module Blocklist
cves.how_introduced: The blamed commit added `extract_identifier_from_ast_node` which recursively processes `ast.Call` nodes to extract identifiers. This implementation did not enforce strict validation on the resulting module strings, allowing attackers to craft pickle payloads that resolve to dangerous modules (like cProfile) which were not correctly caught by the existing blocklist logic.
cves.root_cause: The application's blocklist for unsafe pickle deserialization could be bypassed by using the cProfile.run() function, allowing arbitrary code execution.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The core vulnerability fixed in eb299b45 is the exact-match dangerous-module check in `Pickled.unsafe_imports()` (`node.module in (...)`), which let submodules like `os.path` evade detection; blame shows that line predates the suspected BIC and comes from 670b280e (2022). `5e054ddc` did introduce `if not module.isidentifier() or not attr.isidentifier():` in `StackGlobal.run()` (blame line 1160), but before that commit the code simply cast non-strings and continued, so this was a new validation path rather than the original incomplete blocklist.

cves.description: OpenClaw versions prior to 2026.2.12 fail to validate the sessionFile path parameter, allowing authenticated gateway clients to write transcript data to arbitrary locations on the host filesystem. Attackers can supply a sessionFile path outside the sessions directory to create files and append data repeatedly, potentially causing configuration corruption or denial of service.
cves.how_introduced: The blamed commit modified `resolveSessionTranscriptPath` to dynamically construct a filename using the `sessionId` parameter and then passed it directly to `path.join` without any validation or sanitization, allowing directory traversal sequences in the `sessionId` to escape the intended directory.
cves.root_cause: The application failed to sanitize user-provided session IDs before using them to construct file system paths, allowing attackers to escape the intended session directory via directory traversal sequences (e.g., ../).

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The fix’s security-relevant change is in `resolveSessionFilePath`: before the fix it returned `entry?.sessionFile?.trim()` verbatim, with no containment check, enabling arbitrary-path writes. That behavior was already present before 8da4f259: at 8da4f259’s parent (`5400766b`), `src/config/sessions.ts` already had the same raw `candidate ? candidate : resolveSessionTranscriptPath(...)` logic, and blame shows it was introduced by `7e5cef29` (“Threads: add Slack/Discord thread sessions”). agent_verdicts.reasoning: The suspected commit 0a7af782 did not introduce the security-relevant code fixed for CVE-2026-28508. At commit 0a7af782, `blame_lines` on Idno/Core/Session.php shows the critical pre-fix line `setIsAPIRequest(true)` is attributed to be979a0f3, not 0a7af782; 0a7af782 only owns nearby header-variable lines (`$apiUsername`, `$apiSignature`).

`get_file_at_commit` for Session.php at 0a7af782's parent (5cd4860868b2) already contains the same vulnerable unconditional `setIsAPIRequest(true)` before signature verification, proving the bug predated the suspected commit.

0a7af782 adds `version.idno` and does not modify `Idno/Pages/Service/Web/UrlUnfurl.php` or `Idno/Entities/UnfurledUrl.php`; blame shows the unfurl endpoint/token gatekeeper and the URL fetch path were introduced years earlier (2017/2019).

cves.description: Graphiti vulnerable to Cypher Injection via unsanitized node_labels in search filters

cves.root_cause: The application concatenated user-supplied node labels directly into Cypher queries without proper sanitization or parameterization, allowing an attacker to inject malicious Cypher syntax.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: 99923c03 does own the `search_ops.py` lines that 7d65d5e7 hardened with `validate_group_ids`, but that is only one auxiliary hardening hunk from a broader security commit. The actual CVE-described Cypher-injection bug is the unsanitized `node_labels` interpolation in `graphiti_core/search/search_filters.py`, and blame shows those lines were introduced earlier by 088029a8 (`node label filters`) and later reshaped by 8802b7db (`Add support for Kuzu`). Before 99923c03, `graphiti_core/search/search_utils.py` was already calling those vulnerable constructors, and it already contained the same unsanitized fulltext/group-id query-building pattern, so 99923c03 is best understood as a refactor/duplication of preexisting behavior rather than the introducer of this CVE.
evidence: Blame on `graphiti_core/search/search_filters.py` at the fixed `node_labels` query-construction lines shows `if filters.node_labels is not None` from 088029a8 and the unsafe `node_label_filter = 'n:' + node_labels` / `... ' AND m:' + node_labels` lines from 8802b7db, not 99923c03.

`get_commit_info` for 99923c03 shows it added `graphiti_core/driver/neo4j/operations/search_ops.py` but did not modify `graphiti_core/search/search_filters.py`, where the node-label injection actually lived.

At pre-BIC commit 1de75264, `graphiti_core/search/search_utils.py` already called `node_search_filter_query_constructor(search_filter, driver.provider)` and already built unsanitized fulltext/group-id filters, proving the vulnerable behavior existed before 99923c03.

Blame on `graphiti_core/driver/neo4j/operations/search_ops.py` lines 61-64 at the fix state points to 99923c03, confirming that commit only introduced the later copied/refactored `group_ids` helper in the new operations file, not the original `node_labels` vulnerability.

cves.description: OpenClaw: Unavailable local auth SecretRefs could fall through to remote credentials in local mode
cves.how_introduced:
1. The blamed commit adds 'perplexity' as a search provider.
2. It introduces the `resolvePerplexityApiKey` function which implements a hardcoded fallback from local configuration to global environment variables (process.env).
3. This logic allows the application to proceed with remote credentials even when local SecretRefs fail to resolve, creating the authentication bypass vulnerability.
cves.root_cause: The application failed to enforce strict credential resolution, allowing the system to silently fallback to remote credentials when configured local authentication (SecretRefs) were missing or unavailable.

bug_commits.author: Kevin Lin
bug_commits.message: feat(web): add Perplexity Sonar as alternative search provider

llm_verdict.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced the Perplexity search provider and its associated credential resolution logic in `src/agents/tools/web-tools.ts`. It explicitly implements a fallback mechanism where `resolvePerplexityApiKey` checks the configuration object first and then silently falls back to `process.env.PERPLEXITY_API_KEY` or other environment variables if the config-based secret is not found. The fix commit refactors this entire tool and introduces `RuntimeWebSearchMetadata` to strictly enforce secret resolution and prevent this insecure fall-through behavior.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: The application failed to enforce strict credential resolution, allowing the system to silently fallback to remote credentials when configured local authentication (SecretRefs) were missing or unavailable.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: Implicit or permissive fallback logic in credential resolution that prioritized remote authentication sources over configured local secret references when the latter failed to load.
llm_verdict.causal_chain:
1. The blamed commit adds 'perplexity' as a search provider.
2. It introduces the `resolvePerplexityApiKey` function which implements a hardcoded fallback from local configuration to global environment variables (process.env).
3. This logic allows the application to proceed with remote credentials even when local SecretRefs fail to resolve, creating the authentication bypass vulnerability.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The fix addresses a later-added eager credential-resolution path: before the fix, `createWebSearchTool()` unconditionally called `resolvePerplexityTransport(perplexityConfig)` during tool construction and used its result for schema/description selection, which is exactly what the fix defers to execution time. Blame on those pre-fix lines in `src/agents/tools/web-search.ts` points to commit `28e46d04e57...` (Mar 8, 2026), not `ff9d069a`. `ff9d069a` only added the original Perplexity provider in the older `src/agents/tools/web-tools.ts`, and that version resolved Perplexity auth lazily inside `execute` with no `resolvePerplexityTransport()` or constructor-time touch of Perplexity credential surfaces, so it did not write the specific vulnerable code fixed here.
evidence: Pre-fix blame on `src/agents/tools/web-search.ts` attributes the security-relevant lines to `28e46d04e57...`: line 1820 (`const perplexityTransport = resolvePerplexityTransport(perplexityConfig);`), lines 1829-1831 (description based on `perplexityTransport.transport`), lines 1846-1849 (schema gets `perplexityTransport.transport`), lines 1851-1854 (reuse of `perplexityTransport`), and lines 722-741 (`resolvePerplexityTransport`).

`git log` for `src/agents/tools/web-search.ts` shows the file was only created later by `568b8ee96ce...` (`refactor: split web tools and docs`); the suspected BIC `ff9d069a33...` did not touch this file at all and only modified `src/agents/tools/web-tools.ts`.

In `ff9d069a`'s `src/agents/tools/web-tools.ts`, `createWebSearchTool()` resolves Perplexity credentials only inside `execute` (`provider === "perplexity" ? resolvePerplexityApiKey(perplexityConfig) : resolveSearchApiKey(search)`) and has no `resolvePerplexityTransport()` call. Later snapshots such as `230fea1ca6...` still keep this lazy execute-time resolution, showing the eager constructor-time pattern was introduced afterward, by `28e46d04`.

cves.description: Langroid has WAF Bypass Leading to RCE in TableChatAgent
cves.how_introduced: 1. The blamed commit introduces the AST-based CommandValidator to sanitize pandas operations. 2. It omits checks for attribute access (visit_Attribute), allowing arbitrary attribute traversal. 3. An attacker can use this omission to access Python internals (e.g., .__class__.__base__) to bypass the WAF and execute arbitrary code.
cves.root_cause: The TableChatAgent in Langroid is vulnerable to a WAF bypass that allows an attacker to execute arbitrary code, likely through insufficient sanitization of inputs passed to a dynamic execution environment.

bug_commits.author: Prasad Chalasani
bug_commits.message: fixes to code-injection mitigation PR #841 (#850)

llm_verdict.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced the `CommandValidator` class and the `pandas_utils.py` logic intended to sanitize pandas commands. However, it failed to implement a `visit_Attribute` method, which allowed attackers to access dangerous dunder attributes like `__globals__` or `__init__` to escape the sandbox and achieve RCE.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: The TableChatAgent in Langroid is vulnerable to a WAF bypass that allows an attacker to execute arbitrary code, likely through insufficient sanitization of inputs passed to a dynamic execution environment.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: The provided diff only shows a version bump; the actual vulnerable pattern typically involves the use of eval() or exec() on unsanitized user input within the TableChatAgent logic.
llm_verdict.causal_chain: 1. The blamed commit introduces the AST-based CommandValidator to sanitize pandas operations. 2. It omits checks for attribute access (visit_Attribute), allowing arbitrary attribute traversal. 3. An attacker can use this omission to access Python internals (e.g., .__class__.__base__) to bypass the WAF and execute arbitrary code.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: Blame shows 0d9e4a7 authored the original `CommandValidator` in `pandas_utils.py`, including the whitelist that allowed `ast.Attribute` and the lack of any `visit_Attribute()` check that the fix later adds. However, history shows this commit was a hardening follow-up, not the origin of the dangerous execution path: its predecessor 36e7e7db had already changed `TableChatAgent` to run user expressions with raw Python `eval(exprn, {}, local_vars)` and had no sanitizer at all. In 0d9e4a7, the project added `sanitize_command()` plus the `full_eval` flag and wired sanitization into `TableChatAgent`, so this is best classified as an insufficient mitigation that the fix strengthened, not the true vulnerability-introducing commit.
evidence: Fix commit 30abbc1 says the bypass existed because there was 'no validation of attribute access' and patches `langroid/utils/pandas_utils.py` by adding `visit_Attribute()` to block dunder/private attributes.

Blame on pre-fix `pandas_utils.py` attributes/method-call logic points to 0d9e4a7, including `ALLOWED_NODES = (... ast.Attribute, ...)` and the surrounding validator code, confirming that commit authored the incomplete sanitizer.
The parent state before 0d9e4a7 (commit 7bfcc614 for `pandas_utils.py`) had no sanitizer at all; 0d9e4a7 newly added `CommandValidator`/`sanitize_command` as a security control.

But `TableChatAgent` was already evaluating expressions before 0d9e4a7: commit 36e7e7db shows `eval_result = eval(exprn, {}, local_vars)` with no `sanitize_command` present, while 0d9e4a7 changed that to `if not self.config.full_eval: exprn = sanitize_command(exprn)` before `eval(...)`.

`git log` for `table_chat_agent.py` shows 36e7e7db ('harden table_chat_agent') immediately precedes 0d9e4a7, supporting that 0d9e4a7 was a follow-up mitigation commit rather than the initial introduction of the risky eval path.

cves.description: The Scratch Channel Has Potential Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) Vulnerability
cves.how_introduced: 1. The blamed commit modified the profile template to include user-controlled data in the src attribute. 2. It applied escapeHtml() to user.pfp, which fails to sanitize against 'javascript:' protocol URIs. 3. This established the vulnerable pattern where an attacker could provide a malicious URI as their profile picture to execute arbitrary JavaScript.
cves.root_cause: User input such as the page title, username, and profile picture URL were interpolated directly into HTML templates without proper escaping or URL validation, allowing execution of arbitrary JavaScript.

bug_commits.author: snoopythe3
bug_commits.message: Potential fix for code scanning alert no. 8: Reflected cross-site scripting

llm_verdict.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced the use of `escapeHtml` on the `user.pfp` attribute within an `` tag's `src` attribute. While this prevents standard HTML injection, it is insufficient for URI-based attributes as it allows `javascript:` URIs, which is a core part of the identified XSS vulnerability that the fix commit eventually addressed by implementing strict URL validation.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: User input such as the page title, username, and profile picture URL were interpolated directly into HTML templates without proper escaping or URL validation, allowing execution of arbitrary JavaScript.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: and (which allowed javascript: URIs)
llm_verdict.causal_chain: 1. The blamed commit modified the profile template to include user-controlled data in the src attribute. 2. It applied escapeHtml() to user.pfp, which fails to sanitize against 'javascript:' protocol URIs. 3. This established the vulnerable pattern where an attacker could provide a malicious URI as their profile picture to execute arbitrary JavaScript.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The final fix closes XSS by escaping the page `<title>`, validating/sanitizing `username` and `pfp` at creation and lookup, and replacing `escapeHtml()` with a URL allowlist for the `img src` attribute. In the pre-fix file, blame shows the core vulnerable patterns were introduced earlier in 85c0995b2791cec9d6f6ed655786299a47ec1167: raw `${title}` in `htmlWrapper`, raw stored `username`/`pfp`, and the unsanitized `res.send(htmlWrapper(`${user.username}...`))` calls. The suspected commit 941c54de24c7f86461b9aa518c29d72c62f11646 only added `escape-html` to several already-existing output sinks, so it was an incomplete mitigation rather than the commit that introduced the vulnerability.
evidence: `api/users.js` at 85c0995b2791cec9d6f6ed655786299a47ec1167 already contains the vulnerable patterns later fixed: `<title>${title}...`, storing raw `username`/`pfp`, raw `users.find(u => u.username === req.params.username)`, and `res.send(htmlWrapper(`${user.username}'s Profile`, ...))`.

Blame on the pre-fix file (`a8fca783a36654116fb8db1a2cfa2484b402e275`) attributes line 11 (`<title>${title}...`), lines 43-45 (`username`, `pfp`), line 55 (raw param lookup), and line 69 (raw title composition) to 85c0995, not to 941c54de.

The suspected BIC diff 941c54de shows only added `const escapeHtml = require('escape-html');` and wrapped existing outputs like `user.pfp`, `user.username`, followers/following entries, and href pieces with `escapeHtml()`.

The fix diff 90b39eb mainly strengthens/changes those controls: `escapeAttribute()` for `src`, `escapeHtml(title)` in `htmlWrapper`, and sanitization of `username`/`pfp` before storage and route matching. That matches an insufficient earlier mitigation, not bug introduction.

cves.description: Karakeep's Reddit plugin content bypasses DOMPurify sanitization, enabling stored XSS
cves.how_introduced: The blamed commit introduced `parseHtmlSubprocess.ts` which extracts `readableContentHtml` from metascraper. By failing to sanitize this specific output path with DOMPurify, it allowed plugins (like the Reddit plugin) to inject arbitrary HTML into the final readable content, leading to stored XSS.
cves.root_cause: The Reddit plugin failed to properly sanitize HTML output from its metascraper, allowing malicious content to bypass DOMPurify filters and execute arbitrary JavaScript.

bug_commits.author: Mohamed Bassem
bug_commits.message: feat: run metascraper & readability parsing in a subprocess

By failing to sanitize this specific output path with DOMPurify, it allowed plugins (like the Reddit plugin) to inject arbitrary HTML into the final readable content, leading to stored XSS.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The fix addresses a missing DOMPurify pass on the direct `meta.readableContentHtml` path: plugin-provided HTML was copied straight into `readableContent`, while the fallback `extractReadableContent` path did sanitize. Although blame on `apps/workers/scripts/parseHtmlSubprocess.ts` points to 7a100672 because that commit created the file, 7a100672 was a refactor/extraction: its diff moved the same unsanitized `meta.readableContentHtml ? { content: meta.readableContentHtml } : null` logic out of `crawlerWorker.ts` into the new subprocess file. Pre-BIC history shows the vulnerable pattern already existed in `apps/workers/workers/crawlerWorker.ts` before 7a100672, and blame there traces the logic to commit f5c32d940ea6c2a6da6c225c2de1eba13b49f9e0, which also introduced the Reddit plugin's `readableContentHtml` output.
evidence: Fix commit ba3db953 changes only `apps/workers/scripts/parseHtmlSubprocess.ts` to sanitize `meta.readableContentHtml` with DOMPurify before assigning `readableContent`.

Blame on the vulnerable lines in `parseHtmlSubprocess.ts` at lines 127-131 attributes them to 7a100672, but `git log` for that file shows it was newly created by 7a100672 and only later fixed by ba3db953, indicating possible refactor/blame drift.

The 7a100672 diff for `apps/workers/workers/crawlerWorker.ts` removes the same vulnerable logic from the old location: `let readableContent ... = meta.readableContentHtml ? { content: meta.readableContentHtml } : null; if (!readableContent) { extractReadableContent(...) }`, showing the commit moved existing behavior into the subprocess.

In pre-BIC commit 36309aecbaab4ec94791fd1fce91676b30e6bd7c, `crawlerWorker.ts` already contained the same unsanitized `meta.readableContentHtml` path, and blame on those lines traces to f5c32d940ea6c2a6da6c225c2de1eba13b49f9e0, not 7a100672.

Commit f5c32d940ea6c2a6da6c225c2de1eba13b49f9e0 both added `readableContentHtml` to `metascraper-reddit.ts` and changed `crawlerWorker.ts` from always calling sanitized `extractReadableContent(htmlContent, ...)` to trusting `meta.readableContentHtml` directly.

cves.description: maxminddb's `Reader::open_mmap` unsoundly marks unsafe memmap operation as safe
cves.how_introduced: 1. The blamed commit adds the `open_mmap` function to `src/reader.rs`. 2. It defines the function as `pub fn open_mmap`, making it part of the safe public API. 3. The implementation uses `unsafe { MmapOptions::new().map(...) }` internally but does not propagate the `unsafe` requirement to the caller, creating a soundness hole.
cves.root_cause: The Reader::open_mmap function was incorrectly marked as safe despite performing memory-mapped file operations that can lead to undefined behavior, which is a soundness issue in Rust.

bug_commits.author: Gregory Oschwald
bug_commits.message: Reorganize code into modern module structure

llm_verdict.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced the `open_mmap` function and marked it as a public safe function (`pub fn`). Because memory-mapping a file can lead to undefined behavior if the file is modified externally, Rust safety guidelines require such functions to be marked `unsafe`, which this commit failed to do.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: The Reader::open_mmap function was incorrectly marked as safe despite performing memory-mapped file operations that can lead to undefined behavior, which is a soundness issue in Rust.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: pub fn open_mmap(...) { ... }
llm_verdict.causal_chain: 1. The blamed commit adds the `open_mmap` function to `src/reader.rs`. 2. It defines the function as `pub fn open_mmap`, making it part of the safe public API. 3. The implementation uses `unsafe { MmapOptions::new().map(...) }` internally but does not propagate the `unsafe` requirement to the caller, creating a soundness hole.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The fix makes `Reader::open_mmap` an `unsafe fn` because it exposed `memmap2::MmapOptions::map()`—an unsafe operation—as a safe public API. Although blame on the pre-fix `src/reader.rs` points to 11fc4300, that is blame drift from the module split: the same safe `open_mmap` wrapper and unsafe mmap call already existed unchanged in `src/maxminddb/lib.rs` at the parent of 11fc. Commit 11fc4300 was a reorganization/move commit (“No public API changes”), so it relocated the vulnerable code rather than introducing it.
evidence: Fix commit 98f0e4f changes `pub fn open_mmap` to `pub unsafe fn open_mmap` and removes the internal `unsafe { ... }` wrapper, showing the vulnerability is the safe exposure of `MmapOptions::map()` in `src/reader.rs`.
Blame on the pre-fix file state (`src/reader.rs:75-77` at commit 14e78721) attributes the vulnerable lines to 11fc4300 only because that commit created `src/reader.rs`.

At 11fc4300's parent (commit 4a0e7599), `src/maxminddb/lib.rs:518-520` already contains the same code: `pub fn open_mmap...` with `let mmap = unsafe { MmapOptions::new().map(&file_read) }.map_err(MaxMindDbError::Mmap)?;`.
Commit 11fc4300’s message says it reorganized code into modules with 'No public API changes', consistent with a pure move/refactor rather than a security-relevant rewrite.

Blame on the pre-11fc location (`src/maxminddb/lib.rs:518-520` at 4a0e7599) traces the lines to earlier commits (`e523f86b` / `2d8af90c`), and older history shows `open_mmap` itself was added in 2018 (`92b22634` / `71e42022`), well before 11fc4300.

bug_commits.author: ZacharyZcR
bug_commits.message: Fix SSH key upload and credential editing issues

llm_verdict.reasoning: The vulnerability is a Stored XSS and LFI in the File Manager. The blamed commit modifies the credential editor for SSH keys and API request configurations, which are entirely unrelated to file manager functionality or rendering file paths.
llm_verdict.vuln_type: Stored XSS and Local File Inclusion (LFI)
llm_verdict.vuln_description: The application fails to sanitize user-supplied file names or content in the File Manager, allowing attackers to inject malicious scripts (XSS) or manipulate file paths to access unauthorized local files (LFI) within the Electron environment.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: Directly rendering or processing user-controlled file paths/names without validation or sanitization in the file management interface.

cves.description: OpenClaw Twitch allowFrom is not enforced in optional plugin, unauthorized chat users can trigger agent pipeline

bug_commits.author: jaydenfyi
bug_commits.message: refactor: improve Twitch plugin code quality and fix all tests

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The fix adds a missing `return { allowed: false, ... }` immediately after the `allowFrom` check, changing `allowFrom` from a soft bypass into a hard allowlist. In the pre-fix code, if `allowFrom` was set and the sender was not listed, execution fell through to `allowedRoles` or the final `allowed: true` fallback, allowing unauthorized users to reach `processTwitchMessage` in `monitor.ts`. Blame on the vulnerable lines in the pre-fix file points to `f5c90f0e5c7`, and that commit created `extensions/twitch/src/access-control.ts` as a new file; the file did not exist in the parent commit. The original tests added in the same commit explicitly expected this insecure behavior (`allows users not in allowlist via fallback (open access)`), confirming the BIC authored the vulnerable logic rather than merely moving it.
evidence: Fix commit 8c7901c adds a new denial return after the `allowFrom.includes(senderId)` success case in `extensions/twitch/src/access-control.ts`, showing the bug was missing enforcement of non-membership in `allowFrom`.
`blame_lines` at pre-fix revision 230ca789e231f16b017ef7a000d5e5be99b26720 attributes the vulnerable `allowFrom` block (lines 51-69) to `f5c90f0e5c7a12285ceea6c3102666a7b904b16f`.

`get_commit_diff` for `f5c90f0e5c7` shows `extensions/twitch/src/access-control.ts` was introduced as a new file containing the fall-through logic; `get_file_at_commit` at parent commit `c5ffc11df52f03d5b64ae5e6a381412fac35ee7b` reports the file did not exist.

Pre-fix `monitor.ts` calls `checkTwitchAccessControl(...)` and then, when `access.allowed` is true, invokes `processTwitchMessage(...)`, so the flawed allowlist logic directly gates the agent pipeline.
Pre-fix tests from the same BIC explicitly asserted the insecure behavior: `allows users not in allowlist via fallback (open access)` and `allows user with role even if not in allowlist`.
