cves.description: Mattermost Confluence Plugin is Missing Authentication for Critical Function

bug_commits.author: kshitij katiyar
bug_commits.message: Update server/confluence_server.go

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The suspected commit c9b50706 did not add or remove any authentication logic; it only changed the handler signature and error/logging text in `renderAtlassianConnectJSON`. Blame on the pre-fix file shows the actual access-control check `verifyHTTPSecret(conf.Secret, r.FormValue("secret"))` traces back to the original 2020 introduction commit (737d5bb7), while c9b50706 owns only the surrounding log/error lines. The same unauthenticated endpoint behavior also existed before c9b50706 in `bfaf6de1`, so this is blame drift/trivial-touch attribution rather than true bug introduction.
evidence: At fix parent `de0a3d4`, blame for `verifyHTTPSecret(conf.Secret, r.FormValue("secret"))` in `server/atlassian_connect.go` points to 737d5bb7 (2020), not c9b50706; c9b50706 only blames for `LogWarn(...)` and `http.Error(w, "invalid secret", status)`.
`get_file_at_commit` for pre-BIC commit `bfaf6de1` shows `renderAtlassianConnectJSON` already existed with the same secret-based gate and same unauthenticated endpoint semantics before c9b50706.

Original introduction commit 737d5bb7 created `server/controller/atlassian_connect.go` with `RequiresAuth: false` and the same `verifyHTTPSecret(...)` check, demonstrating the relevant access-control pattern predates c9b50706 by years.

The supposed fix commit c32e004 only changes logging/error messages and adds a 500 response on bundle-path failure; it does not patch authentication logic, so the c9b50706 lines it touches are incidental cleanup rather than the missing-auth root cause.

cves.description: @cyanheads/git-mcp-server vulnerable to command injection in several tools

bug_commits.author: Casey Hand
bug_commits.message: Update src/mcp-server/tools/gitAdd/logic.ts

llm_verdict.reasoning: The blamed commit modifies how file paths starting with a dash are handled, but it does not introduce the vulnerable `exec` call or the string concatenation that leads to command injection. The vulnerability was already present in the code prior to this commit.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: The application used child_process.exec to execute Git commands, concatenating unsanitized user input directly into the shell command string. This allowed attackers to execute arbitrary system commands by including shell metacharacters in the input.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: exec(`git ${command} ${userInput}`)

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The fix addresses a shell-command injection pattern: `child_process.exec` was used on a constructed command string, and the patch replaces that with `execFile` plus an argument array. In the pre-fix file, blame shows the security-relevant lines `const execAsync = promisify(exec);`, `const command = ...`, and `await execAsync(command)` all trace to the initial file creation commit `ddf2f0d3`, not to `7878f97d`. The suspected BIC only changed how filenames beginning with `-` were handled (prefixing `./` instead of rejecting them), and the same vulnerable `exec`-with-interpolated-command pattern already existed in its parent `0c26189e`, so this commit did not introduce the CVE's root vulnerability.
evidence: Fix commit 0dbd6995 replaces `exec`/`execAsync(command)` and shell-built `const command = \`git -C "${targetPath}" add -- ${filesArg}\`` with `execFile` and explicit args in `src/mcp-server/tools/gitAdd/logic.ts`.

Blame at pre-fix state (`fc9fb73b`, the commit before the fix) attributes `const execAsync = promisify(exec);` (line 12) and the vulnerable execution lines `const command = ...` / `await execAsync(command)` (lines 166-169) to `ddf2f0d3`, not `7878f97d`.
The suspected BIC 7878f97d changes only dash-leading filename handling in the quoting block (`if startsWith('-') throw` -> prefix `./`), per its diff.

The file at `7878f97d^` (`0c26189e`) already contains the same `exec` import, `execAsync`, shell command construction, and `execAsync(command)` call, proving the vulnerable pattern predates the suspected BIC.

cves.description: mcp-server-kubernetes vulnerable to command injection in several tools

bug_commits.author: Casey Hand
bug_commits.message: Update src/mcp-server/tools/gitAdd/logic.ts

llm_verdict.reasoning: The blamed commit modifies how file paths starting with a dash are handled, but it does not introduce the vulnerable `exec` call or the string concatenation that leads to command injection. The vulnerability was already present in the code prior to this commit.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: The application used child_process.exec to execute Git commands, concatenating unsanitized user input directly into the shell command string. This allowed attackers to execute arbitrary system commands by including shell metacharacters in the input.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: exec(`git ${command} ${userInput}`)

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The fix patches an unsafe execution pattern: building a shell command string and running it via `child_process.exec`, then replaces that with `execFile` argument passing. In the pre-fix file, blame shows the security-critical lines `const execAsync = promisify(exec);`, `const command = \`git -C "${targetPath}" add -- ${filesArg}\`;`, and `await execAsync(command);` all come from the initial rewrite commit `ddf2f0d3`, not from `7878f97d`. The parent of the suspected BIC (`0c26189e`) already had the same vulnerable shell-command construction; `7878f97d` only changed dash-prefixed filename handling (`-foo` -> `./-foo`) and did not introduce the shell injection pattern.
evidence: `git blame` on `src/mcp-server/tools/gitAdd/logic.ts` at pre-fix commit `fc9fb73b` attributes line 12 (`const execAsync = promisify(exec);`) and lines 166/169 (`const command = ... ${filesArg}` and `await execAsync(command);`) to `ddf2f0d317b8ff1dbe5a78d1b9332a0b68e6ecaa`.

`get_file_at_commit` for the suspected BIC's parent `0c26189e654cb71847303991720b78c2201890e1` already contains the vulnerable pattern: `exec`, `filesArg` interpolation into a command string, and `execAsync(command)`.

The diff for `7878f97d211447da4b7c87f6d51ee79dbf2e0fbe` only changes file-argument preprocessing for dash-prefixed names; it does not introduce `exec`, the shell command string, or `execAsync(command)`.

The fix commit message explicitly states the mitigation is replacing all `child_process.exec` uses with `execFile`, confirming the vulnerability is the shell-execution pattern that predated `7878f97d`.

cves.description: Mattermost Confluence Plugin has Missing Authorization vulnerability

bug_commits.author: kshitij katiyar
bug_commits.message: Update server/confluence_server.go

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The suspected commit c9b50706 did not introduce the access-control behavior in `renderAtlassianConnectJSON`; the security-relevant logic (`verifyHTTPSecret(conf.Secret, r.FormValue("secret"))` on an unauthenticated descriptor endpoint) was already present before c9 and traces back to the original 2020 implementation. Blame at the pre-fix state shows c9 only authored the later-edited logging/error-response lines, while the authorization check itself is blamed to older commits. The parent of c9 (`bfaf6de1`) already contains the same endpoint, same secret check, and same unauthenticated route structure, so c9 was at most incidental cleanup / error-message hardening, not the bug introducer for a missing-authorization flaw.
evidence: `blame_lines` on `server/atlassian_connect.go` at pre-fix commit `de0a3d4` shows line 23 (`verifyHTTPSecret(conf.Secret, r.FormValue("secret"))`) comes from `737d5bb7` (2020), not from `c9b50706`; c9 only owns lines 24-25, 46-47, and 53-54 (logging/error text).

`get_file_at_commit` for c9's parent `bfaf6de1` shows the same handler already existed before c9, including the same secret verification and endpoint definition; c9 only changed responses from raw errors to generic messages and added logging.

`get_commit_diff` for c9 confirms its changes in `server/atlassian_connect.go` were limited to adding `p *Plugin`, log calls, and different `http.Error` messages; it did not add or remove authorization checks.

The file history shows the endpoint originated in commit `737d5bb7` (`MI-814: Render a template for atlassian-connect.json file`) with `RequiresAuth/RequiresAdmin: false` and the same `verifyHTTPSecret` gate, indicating the relevant pattern predates c9 by years.

cves.description: Mattermost Confluence Plugin has Missing Authorization vulnerability

bug_commits.author: kshitij katiyar
bug_commits.message: Update server/confluence_server.go

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The blamed commit c9b50706 touched `renderAtlassianConnectJSON`, but it did not introduce the security-relevant behavior; it actually replaced raw `err.Error()` responses with generic messages and added logging. In the pre-fix state, blame shows the core secret check `verifyHTTPSecret(conf.Secret, r.FormValue("secret"))` still came from the original 2020 implementation, and the file state before c9 already had the same unauthenticated/secret-gated endpoint behavior. The later fix c32e004 mostly refines logging and response text, so c9 is at most an intermediate hardening step, not the introducer of the missing-authorization issue.
evidence: `blame_lines` on `server/atlassian_connect.go` at pre-fix commit `de0a3d4c...` shows line 23 (`verifyHTTPSecret(conf.Secret, r.FormValue("secret"))`) still blamed to 2020 commit `737d5bb7`, while only the generic error/logging lines 24-25, 46-47, and 53-54 are blamed to `c9b50706`.

`get_file_at_commit` for pre-c9 version `bfaf6de1...:server/atlassian_connect.go` shows the same endpoint logic already existed before c9, including the secret check and error paths; c9 merely changed `http.Error(w, err.Error(), ...)` to generic messages.

The original introduction commit `737d5bb7` created `server/controller/atlassian_connect.go` with the same `verifyHTTPSecret(...)` gate and an explicitly unauthenticated endpoint (`RequiresAuth/RequiresAdmin: false` semantics), so the authorization pattern predates c9 by years.

`get_commit_diff` for c9 on `server/atlassian_connect.go` shows only response/logging changes, and `get_commit_diff` for `server/controller.go` at c9 produced no auth-related changes at all; the auth wrapper (`IsAuthenticated`) was only added later in `de0a3d4c`, with this endpoint explicitly left `false`.

cves.description: Mattermost Confluence Plugin has Missing Authorization vulnerability

bug_commits.author: kshitij katiyar
bug_commits.message: Update server/confluence_server.go

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The June 2025 fix commit does not change authorization logic in `renderAtlassianConnectJSON`; it only adjusts logging, response text, and adds a 500 response on bundle-path failure. Blame on the pre-fix file shows the auth-relevant behavior (`verifyHTTPSecret(...)`, the unauthenticated endpoint, and generation of the descriptor containing `SharedSecret`) predates `c9b50706`, with core lines tracing back to the original 2020 implementation and the later auth-framework commit merely making the endpoint explicitly unauthenticated. `c9b50706` only touched incidental error-handling lines in that function, so it did not introduce the missing-authorization condition.
evidence: `c32e0043` only changes log/error messages in `server/atlassian_connect.go`; it does not modify `verifyHTTPSecret`, endpoint auth gating, or request handling semantics.

Blame at `de0a3d4c` (state before the fix) shows line 23 `if status, err := verifyHTTPSecret(conf.Secret, r.FormValue("secret")); ...` and the descriptor-building lines trace to `737d5bb7` (2020), while `c9b50706` is blamed only for lines 24-25, 46-47, and 53-54, which are logging/response-message changes.
The file before `c9b50706` (`bfaf6de1`) already had the same unauthenticated `/atlassian-connect.json` handler and the same secret check, proving the pattern existed before the suspected BIC.

The original 2020 file `server/controller/atlassian_connect.go` already declared `RequiresAuth: false` and used the same `verifyHTTPSecret` gate, so the missing-authorization pattern long predates `c9b50706`.

cves.description: Coder's privilege escalation vulnerability could lead to a cross workspace compromise

bug_commits.author: Danielle Maywood
bug_commits.message: chore(agent/agentcontainers): disable project autostart by default (#19114)

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The security fix is the opt-in guard added around devcontainer autostart: commit e6ec95757ab9 wraps the `ReadConfig`/`AutoStart` logic in `if api.discoveryAutostart` and adds a new CLI/env flag whose default is `false`, which disables the risky behavior by default. Blame on the vulnerable pre-fix lines in `agent/agentcontainers/api.go` attributes the unconditional autostart logic to 66cf90c7360, not to e6ec95757ab9. History also shows the autostart block did not exist before 66cf90c, and e6ec is a cherry-pick/backport commit that bundles this mitigation with unrelated changes, so it did not introduce the vulnerability.
evidence: `agent/agentcontainers/api.go` in e6ec95757ab9 changes the vulnerable block from unconditional `ReadConfig`/`AutoStart` handling to `if api.discoveryAutostart { ... }`, i.e. it restricts behavior rather than adding it.
`cli/agent.go` in e6ec95757ab9 adds `--devcontainers-discovery-autostart-enable` / `CODER_AGENT_DEVCONTAINERS_DISCOVERY_AUTOSTART_ENABLE` with `Default: "false"`, making autostart opt-in.

`blame_lines` on the vulnerable lines just before the fix (`agent/agentcontainers/api.go` lines 545-557 in commit cc4f8da6e144...) attributes the unconditional autostart code to 66cf90c73600e29ad9b818f52a70d79005618624, not to e6ec95757ab9.

`get_file_at_commit` shows the autostart logic is absent in 25d70ce7bc37 (before 66cf90c) and present in 66cf90c, while `git_log` from e6ec on `agent/agentcontainers/api.go` shows 66cf90c as the prior history entry, meaning the vulnerable pattern already existed before e6ec.

decomposed_commits.message: feat: validate presets on template import (#18844)
chore(agent/agentcontainers): disable project autostart by default (#19114)
ci: set valid xcode version in release script (#19143)
docs: document how to start a remote MCP Coder server (#19150)
docs: add OAuth2 provider experimental feature documentation (#19165)

cves.description: quic-go HTTP/3 QPACK Header Expansion DoS

bug_commits.author: Marten Seemann
bug_commits.message: qlog invalid trailers

llm_verdict.reasoning: The blamed commit simply adds a qlog call to decodeTrailers in http3/conn.go upon error. It does not introduce the header parsing logic or the missing size limits that led to the resource exhaustion vulnerability.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: The HTTP/3 implementation failed to enforce a limit on the total size of decompressed QPACK headers, allowing an attacker to send highly compressed headers that expand into a large amount of memory, leading to resource exhaustion.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: requestFromHeaders(decodeFromSlice(headers), nil)

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The fix adds a new decompressed-header size accounting check in `parseHeaders` and threads a `sizeLimit` argument through `requestFromHeaders` / `updateResponseFromHeaders`. Although blame on the pre-fix file points those current lines to `b7886d5c`, the same vulnerable behavior already existed before that commit: at `f1c7a5df` (`b7886d5c^`), `parseHeaders(headers []qpack.HeaderField, ...)` iterated over decoded headers with no decompressed-size limit, and the request/response paths only enforced `hf.Length > maxHeaderBytes` on the compressed HEADERS frame. `b7886d5c` mainly adapted the code from `DecodeFull` to the new `Decode` API and preserved the existing missing-check logic, so this is blame drift / refactoring rather than the true introduction of the vulnerability.
evidence: At fix-parent `4cdebbea6`, blame for `http3/headers.go:47-61`, `170-171`, and `244-245` points to `b7886d5c`, showing why it was suspected.

In `b7886d5c^` (`f1c7a5df`), `http3/headers.go` already had `func parseHeaders(headers []qpack.HeaderField, isRequest bool)` looping over all decoded headers with no field-list/decompressed-size accounting.

In `b7886d5c^` (`f1c7a5df`), `http3/server.go` and `http3/http_stream.go` only checked `hf.Length > maxHeaderBytes` before decoding, i.e. compressed HEADERS frame size, not expanded QPACK header size.

The `b7886d5c` diff for `http3/headers.go` is an API migration from `[]qpack.HeaderField` / `DecodeFull` to `qpack.DecodeFunc` / `Decode`, keeping the header-processing logic materially the same with respect to size limits.

bug_commits.author: Marten Seemann
bug_commits.message: qlog invalid trailers

llm_verdict.reasoning: The blamed commit only adds a logging call (maybeQlogInvalidHeadersFrame) to the trailer decoding logic. It does not touch the request header decompression process, the QPACK decoder invocation, or the size limit logic that was identified as the root cause in the vulnerability analysis.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: The QPACK header decompression process lacked adequate size limits, allowing an attacker to send specially crafted headers that consume excessive memory and cause resource exhaustion.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: Unbounded header decompression during QPACK decoding, which allows processing of larger-than-expected header blocks.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The fix adds a new decompressed-header size accounting check in `parseHeaders` and threads a `sizeLimit` argument through `requestFromHeaders` / `updateResponseFromHeaders`. evidence: At fix-parent `4cdebbea6`, blame for `http3/headers.go:47-61`, `170-171`, and `244-245` points to `b7886d5c`, showing why it was suspected.

In `b7886d5c^` (`f1c7a5df`), `http3/headers.go` already had `func parseHeaders(headers []qpack.HeaderField, isRequest bool)` looping over all decoded headers with no field-list/decompressed-size accounting.

In `b7886d5c^` (`f1c7a5df`), `http3/server.go` and `http3/http_stream.go` only checked `hf.Length > maxHeaderBytes` before decoding, i.e. compressed HEADERS frame size, not expanded QPACK header size.

The `b7886d5c` diff for `http3/headers.go` is an API migration from `[]qpack.HeaderField` / `DecodeFull` to `qpack.DecodeFunc` / `Decode`, keeping the header-processing logic materially the same with respect to size limits.

bug_commits.author: Marten Seemann
bug_commits.message: qlog invalid trailers

llm_verdict.reasoning: The blamed commit modifies `http3/conn.go` to add a qlog call for error handling in `decodeTrailers`. The vulnerability involves missing size limits in `http3/stream.go` during header decompression, which is logically and geographically distinct from the change made in the blamed commit.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: The QPACK header decompression process lacked adequate size limits, allowing an attacker to send specially crafted headers that consume excessive memory and cause resource exhaustion.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: Unbounded header decompression during QPACK decoding, which allows processing of larger-than-expected header blocks.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The fix adds a new decompressed-header size accounting check in `parseHeaders` and threads a `sizeLimit` argument through `requestFromHeaders` / `updateResponseFromHeaders`. Although blame on the pre-fix file points those current lines to `b7886d5c`, the same vulnerable behavior already existed before that commit: at `f1c7a5df` (`b7886d5c^`), `parseHeaders(headers []qpack.HeaderField, ...)` iterated over decoded headers with no decompressed-size limit, and the request/response paths only enforced `hf.Length > maxHeaderBytes` on the compressed HEADERS frame. `b7886d5c` mainly adapted the code from `DecodeFull` to the new `Decode` API and preserved the existing missing-check logic, so this is blame drift / refactoring rather than the true introduction of the vulnerability.
evidence: At fix-parent `4cdebbea6`, blame for `http3/headers.go:47-61`, `170-171`, and `244-245` points to `b7886d5c`, showing why it was suspected.

In `b7886d5c^` (`f1c7a5df`), `http3/headers.go` already had `func parseHeaders(headers []qpack.HeaderField, isRequest bool)` looping over all decoded headers with no field-list/decompressed-size accounting.

In `b7886d5c^` (`f1c7a5df`), `http3/server.go` and `http3/http_stream.go` only checked `hf.Length > maxHeaderBytes` before decoding, i.e. compressed HEADERS frame size, not expanded QPACK header size.

The `b7886d5c` diff for `http3/headers.go` is an API migration from `[]qpack.HeaderField` / `DecodeFull` to `qpack.DecodeFunc` / `Decode`, keeping the header-processing logic materially the same with respect to size limits.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The fix’s security-relevant change is removing the derived `Debug` on `Credentials` and replacing it with a custom formatter that masks `secret_key`; the `init_global_action_credentials` error-type cleanup is secondary. Blame on the pre-fix file (`40ad2a6e...`) does point the current `#[derive(..., Debug)]` in `crates/credentials/src/credentials.rs` to `eb33e82b...`, but history shows that exact vulnerable pattern already existed before this commit in `crates/policy/src/auth/credentials.rs`. In `82a6e788...` (the commit before `eb33e82b...`), that older file already had `#[derive(Serialize, Deserialize, Clone, Default, Debug)]` on the same `Credentials` struct, and blame there traces the derive/struct back to even earlier commits (`1c44e2d099...`, `bb2f765e5...`). So `eb33e82b...` is best understood as a refactor/extraction that moved vulnerable code into a new crate/file, not the commit that originally introduced the plaintext-logging behavior.
evidence: At pre-fix state `40ad2a6ea9e6e3fd7d8af92bdcdd266134c481c3`, blame on `crates/credentials/src/credentials.rs` lines 212-215 (`#[derive(..., Debug)] pub struct Credentials { ... secret_key ... }`) attributes them to `eb33e82b56ed11fd12bb39416359d8d60737dc7a`.
The fix commit `2108c4ad...` removes `Debug` derive from `Credentials` and adds a manual `Debug` impl that masks `secret_key`, showing the vulnerable pattern is plaintext formatting of credentials.

In the parent history before `eb33e82b...`, `crates/policy/src/auth/credentials.rs` at `82a6e78845e6ae97f24b0b1b726bd496882e8cbd` already contains the same `#[derive(Serialize, Deserialize, Clone, Default, Debug)] pub struct Credentials { pub secret_key: String, ... }`.

Blame on that pre-BIC file (`82a6e788...`, lines 123-135) traces the derive/struct to earlier commits `1c44e2d0997842480c47d256e77db246654b4c23` and `bb2f765e5de028c58c9ba02e8bfd944461b2f8f7`, confirming the vulnerable formatting existed before `eb33e82b...`.

`eb33e82b...` diff for `crates/policy/src/auth/credentials.rs` removes the inline `Credentials` definition and replaces it with `use rustfs_credentials::Credentials;`, consistent with moving/extracting the existing code rather than inventing the vulnerable `Debug` behavior.

bug_commits.message: Update src/mcp-server/tools/gitAdd/logic.ts

llm_verdict.reasoning: The blamed commit modifies how file paths starting with a dash are handled, but it does not introduce the vulnerable `exec` call or the string concatenation that leads to command injection. The vulnerability was already present in the code prior to this commit.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: The application used child_process.exec to execute Git commands, concatenating unsanitized user input directly into the shell command string. This allowed attackers to execute arbitrary system commands by including shell metacharacters in the input.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: exec(`git ${command} ${userInput}`)

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The security fix addresses shell command injection by replacing `child_process.exec` plus a string-built command (`git -C "${targetPath}" add -- ${filesArg}`) with `execFile` and argument arrays. Those core vulnerable lines in `src/mcp-server/tools/gitAdd/logic.ts` were already introduced in the initial rewrite commit `ddf2f0d3`, and blame on the pre-fix file attributes `const execAsync = promisify(exec)`, the `command` string, and `execAsync(command)` to that earlier commit, not to `7878f97d`. Commit `7878f97d` only changed dash-prefixed file handling from rejecting such paths to prefixing them with `./`, and that same dash-prefix logic is retained in the security fix, indicating `7878f97d` was not the code pattern the fix was patching.
evidence: Fix commit `0dbd6995` states the mitigation is replacing `child_process.exec` with `execFile` to prevent shell metacharacter injection, and in `gitAdd/logic.ts` it removes the string-built `command` and `execAsync(command)` path.
Blame on the pre-fix file (`fc9fb73b`, lines 166 and 169) attributes `const command = `git -C "${targetPath}" add -- ${filesArg}`;` and `await execAsync(command);` to `ddf2f0d3`, not `7878f97d`.
Blame on pre-fix line 12 attributes `const execAsync = promisify(exec);` to `ddf2f0d3`, showing the unsafe shell-exec pattern originated in the initial rewrite commit.
`git log` for `src/mcp-server/tools/gitAdd/logic.ts` shows `7878f97d` sits after `0c26189e` and before no larger merge; `7878f97d` is a small one-file update, not a squash merge.

The file at `7878f97d`'s parent (`0c26189e`) already contains the same vulnerable `exec` + string interpolation pattern; `7878f97d` only changes `if (file.startsWith('-')) throw ...` into `const sanitizedFile = file.startsWith('-') ? './${file}' : file`, and the fix keeps that dash-prefix behavior while only removing shell execution.

cves.description: SillyTavern Web Interface Vulnerable to DNS Rebinding

bug_commits.author: Cohee
bug_commits.message: Move default slash commands to respective module (#4240)

llm_verdict.reasoning: The vulnerability is a server-side DNS Rebinding issue that requires validating the 'Host' header of incoming HTTP requests. The blamed commit (and the provided fix commit) only modifies client-side JavaScript related to UI slash commands, which has nothing to do with server-side request handling or origin validation.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: The application failed to validate the 'Host' header of incoming requests, allowing attackers to bypass same-origin policies via DNS rebinding to interact with the local web interface.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: Lack of Host header validation or origin checking in the web server configuration, allowing arbitrary domains to resolve to localhost and access the interface.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The suspected commit is a refactor/move commit, not the introducer of a DNS-rebinding flaw. The fix commit f9d1279 only wraps slash-command help/labels in translation calls and does not add any Host/Origin validation, CSRF logic, or other rebinding mitigation; the lines blamed to 28189bb are incidental UI/help-text overlaps. Although blame on pre-fix slash-commands.js points the /api block at 28189bb, the same command logic already existed before that commit in public/script.js, and pre-BIC blame traces the underlying implementation to older 2023/2024 commits.
evidence: BIC 28189bb is titled "Move default slash commands to respective module (#4240)" and its diff removes slash-command code from public/script.js while adding it to public/scripts/slash-commands.js, indicating a relocation/refactor rather than new security logic.

Pre-BIC file state at 8449b4bb276e1f396df2b068cfadd5b84b7aee4f shows the same /api, /impersonate, /delchat, /renamechat, /getchatname, /closechat, /tempchat, /panels, /forcesave, /instruct, and /context commands already present in public/script.js before 28189bb.

Blame on pre-BIC public/script.js shows the underlying /api implementation (connectAPISlash) predates 28189bb, with key lines attributed to older commits such as ^edd41989f (2023-07-20), 9176f46caf (2023-12-12), and 5288d81424 (2024-08-20); tempchat also predates 28189bb (17c60ca916, 2024-09-04).

Blame on the pre-fix slash-commands.js shows /api lines now attributed to 28189bb only because that commit moved them into this file; other potentially sensitive commands touched by the translation fix, such as /api-url and /clipboard-get, blame to entirely different earlier commits (7b3d13ac00 / 6625e4036e), not 28189bb.
Fix commit f9d1279's metadata and diff show it is a translation-only change in public/scripts/slash-commands.js and public/scripts/slash-commands/SlashCommand.js, not a DNS-rebinding/security patch.

cves.description: ActualBudget server is Missing Authentication for SimpleFIN and Pluggy AI bank sync endpoints

bug_commits.author: Cursor Agent
bug_commits.message: feat: Add warning for unresolved imports

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The fix for CVE-2026-27584 is the addition of `validateSessionMiddleware` to the Pluggy.ai and SimpleFIN Express apps; the import reformatting and `express.json()` reordering are incidental. Commit `0c95eb483897a2f5ba35962e4525f2149f1c000c` did not add or remove any authentication logic in either file—it only changed import paths to drop `.js` extensions. Blame on the pre-fix Pluggy.ai file attributes the relevant app setup and unauthenticated route definitions (`app.use(express.json())`, `app.use(requestLoggerMiddleware)`, and the `/status` route) to the earlier file-creation commit `d5e2030d2359f9807c47e868694efcf7fed1cdbb`, and the same missing-auth pattern is already present in the file before `0c95` (e.g. at `a508a8705c01b5fa32d659d1c56b2bb4995eec16`).
evidence: Fix commit `ea937d100956ca56689ff852d99c28589e2a7d88` adds `validateSessionMiddleware` to `packages/sync-server/src/app-pluggyai/app-pluggyai.js` and `packages/sync-server/src/app-simplefin/app-simplefin.js`; this is the security-relevant change.
Suspected BIC `0c95eb483897a2f5ba35962e4525f2149f1c000c` changes only import statements in `app-pluggyai.js` and `app-simplefin.js` (removing `.js` suffixes); no middleware or route logic changed.

`blame_lines` on `packages/sync-server/src/app-pluggyai/app-pluggyai.js` at `0c95...` shows lines 9-25—including `app.use(express.json())`, `app.use(requestLoggerMiddleware)`, and the `/status` route—were introduced by `d5e2030d2359f9807c47e868694efcf7fed1cdbb`, not by `0c95...`.

`get_file_at_commit` for `packages/sync-server/src/app-pluggyai/app-pluggyai.js` at earlier commit `a508a8705c01b5fa32d659d1c56b2bb4995eec16` already shows the same unauthenticated setup (`app.use(express.json()); app.use(requestLoggerMiddleware);` with no `validateSessionMiddleware`).

For SimpleFIN, `0c95...` likewise only rewrites imports, while blame at `0c95...` attributes `app.use(express.json())` and `app.use(requestLoggerMiddleware)` to much earlier commits (`e7f6348d9a` / `6a41d28404`).

cves.description: Crawl4AI versions prior to 0.8.0 contain a local file inclusion vulnerability in the Docker API deployment. The /execute_js, /screenshot, /pdf, and /html endpoints accept file:// URLs, allowing unauthenticated remote attackers to read arbitrary files from the server filesystem. An attacker can access sensitive files such as /etc/passwd, /etc/shadow, application configuration files, and environment variables via /proc/self/environ, potentially exposing credentials, API keys, and internal applicat

bug_commits.author: Claude
bug_commits.message: feat: add webhook notifications for crawl job completion

llm_verdict.reasoning: The vulnerable code pattern (executing user-provided code in an insecure environment) is found in the main library extraction logic, whereas the blamed commit introduces a webhook feature for the Docker deployment side. The changes in the blamed commit focus on HTTP endpoints for job status notifications and webhook configuration, which are functionally distinct from the /crawl endpoint's extraction strategy code being fixed in the patch.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: The application's /crawl endpoint accepts a 'hooks' parameter containing Python code that is executed via exec() with a restricted environment that insecurely included the __import__ builtin, allowing attackers to bypass restrictions and execute arbitrary system commands.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: exec(code, {"__builtins__": {"__import__": __import__, ...}})

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The actual LFI fix was introduced in commit f24396c (later merged by a5354f2), which added a new validate_url_scheme() helper in deploy/docker/server.py and called it from /html, /screenshot, /pdf, and /execute_js before passing user input to crawler.arun(). Although blame on the pre-fix file attributes the current vulnerable lines to 7cac008 because that release commit rewrote those functions, the same vulnerable pattern already existed in 7cac008's parent 25507adb: each endpoint still passed body.url directly to crawler.arun() with no scheme validation. In fact, those endpoints were originally added earlier in 5297e362, also without validation, so 7cac008 is blame drift/refactoring rather than the bug-introducing commit.
evidence: Fix commit f24396c added validate_url_scheme() and inserted calls at generate_html, generate_screenshot, generate_pdf, and execute_js in deploy/docker/server.py, showing the security issue was missing URL-scheme validation on attacker-controlled URLs.

At pre-fix state 7cac008, blame attributes body.url -> crawler.arun() lines in these endpoints to 7cac008, but inspecting parent 25507adb shows the same unvalidated calls already existed there (e.g. results = await crawler.arun(url=body.url, config=cfg) in /html, /screenshot, /pdf, /execute_js).
Commit 7cac008's own diff on deploy/docker/server.py only refactored these endpoints (error-handling / crawler acquisition / hooks-related changes); it did not add or remove any URL validation logic.

Earlier commit 5297e362 introduced the /html, /screenshot, /pdf, and /execute_js endpoints in deploy/docker/server.py with direct body.url -> crawler.arun() calls and no scheme checks, predating 7cac008.

Underlying library code in crawl4ai/async_crawler_strategy.py already accepted file:// URLs and opened local files (e.g. elif url.startswith("file://"): ... with open(local_file_path, "r") as f: html = f.read()), so endpoint validation was the missing security control.

bug_commits.author: Marco Beretta
bug_commits.message: fix: replace unconventional 'void e' with explicit comment to clarify intentionally ignored error

llm_verdict.reasoning: The blamed commit modifies error handling logic in the `getListAgentsHandler` function, which is unrelated to the SSRF vulnerability in the Agent Actions or MCP server remote transports. The vulnerability pertains to missing URL validation logic, whereas the blamed commit only changes a comment and the syntax of an empty catch block.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: The application lacks restrictions on target URLs for Agent Actions and MCP Server remote transports, allowing requests to be made to private IP addresses, localhost, and internal domains.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: Unrestricted URL parsing and fetching where user-provided target endpoints are not validated against a blocklist of private/internal network addresses.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The fix is specifically about sanitizing nested `model_parameters` values by calling `removeNullishValues(..., true)` on that object in the agent create/update handlers (and then also cleaning `_modelOptions` in the OpenAI LLM path). In the pre-fix file, the create-side vulnerable code was last touched by earlier commits, not by `8907bd5d`; and the same update-side pattern already existed in `8907bd5d`'s parent as `const { _id, ...updateData } = removeNullishValues(validatedData);`. Commit `8907bd5d` only refactored the update handler to preserve `avatar: null` (`const { avatar: avatarField, _id, ...rest } = validatedData; const updateData = removeNullishValues(rest);`) and did not newly introduce the missing nested cleanup for `model_parameters`.
evidence: Fix commit `24e8a258` adds `if (agentData.model_parameters && typeof agentData.model_parameters === 'object') { agentData.model_parameters = removeNullishValues(agentData.model_parameters, true); }` in `createAgentHandler`, and the analogous block in `updateAgentHandler`.

Blame on the pre-fix create lines points to earlier commits (`a37bf6719c` for `const validatedData = agentCreateSchema.parse(req.body); const { tools = [], ...agentData } = removeNullishValues(validatedData);`), not to `8907bd5d`.
At `8907bd5d`'s parent (`5b1a31ef`), `updateAgentHandler` already contained the vulnerable top-level-only cleanup: `const { _id, ...updateData } = removeNullishValues(validatedData);`.

`8907bd5d` changed update parsing only to support avatar reset: `const { avatar: avatarField, _id, ...rest } = validatedData; const updateData = removeNullishValues(rest); if (avatarField === null) updateData.avatar = avatarField;`—the missing nested `model_parameters` sanitization remained unchanged in substance.
The other security-relevant fix file, `packages/api/src/endpoints/openai/llm.ts`, was not touched by `8907bd5d` at all.

The vulnerable pattern involves executing shell commands, which is not present in either the fix or the blamed commit.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: User-controlled input such as database names were concatenated directly into shell commands without proper escaping, allowing an authenticated attacker to execute arbitrary OS commands.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: $commands[] = "docker exec $this->container_name mongodump ... --db $databaseName ..."

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The fix commit 0ecaa191 is a path-duplication bugfix in `serviceParser()` (`SERVICE_FQDN`/`SERVICE_URL` ending up with duplicated suffixes), not a command-injection fix for file-storage mount paths. Blame on the pre-fix file state (`75381af7422c...`) shows the core vulnerable pattern the fix changes—unconditional `"$fqdn$path"` and `"$url$path"` concatenation—was introduced earlier by `a0bc4dac55...` and `e8892b3d29...`; `56f32d0f87...` only added the adjacent `$fqdnValueForEnv` append as part of a larger refactor. The parent of the suspected BIC (`7fc4a2f7f68a...`) already had the same unconditional path-appending logic, so this bug predated `56f32d0f...`.
evidence: Fix commit message: `fix: prevent SERVICE_FQDN/SERVICE_URL path duplication on FQDN updates`; only changed `bootstrap/helpers/parsers.php` plus tests for path duplication, not shell/file-storage code.

`blame_lines` on `bootstrap/helpers/parsers.php` at `75381af7422cd74c07af29b2cfdb2153baf0f887`, lines 1644-1650: lines 1647 (`$fqdn = "$fqdn$path";`) blame to `a0bc4dac55...`, line 1648 (`$url = "$url$path";`) to `e8892b3d29...`, and only line 1649 (`$fqdnValueForEnv = "$fqdnValueForEnv$path";`) to `56f32d0f87...`.

`get_file_at_commit` for `7fc4a2f7f68a3dbbea545101b39f9fb545226bc5` shows the same pre-existing unconditional block: `if ($path !== '/') { $fqdn = "$fqdn$path"; $url = "$url$path"; }`, proving the pattern existed before the suspected BIC.

cves.description: Windmill Exposes Workspace Slack OAuth Client Secrets to Non-Admin Workspace Members

bug_commits.author: Ruben Fiszel
bug_commits.message: refactor: extract windmill-api-workspaces subcrate (Step 3)

llm_verdict.reasoning: The blamed commit is a large refactoring that moves the workspace settings API logic into a new crate (`windmill-api-workspaces`). It simply preserves the existing vulnerable behavior from the original file rather than introducing the logic itself.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: The application exposed the Slack OAuth client secret in the workspace settings API response to non-admin users.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: Returning sensitive configuration fields (like `slack_oauth_client_secret`) in API responses without checking if the requesting user has admin privileges.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The fix patches `get_settings` so non-admin users no longer receive `slack_oauth_client_secret`, meaning the vulnerable pattern is the plaintext secret being returned from `GET /workspaces/get_settings` without an admin-only filter. Although blame on the pre-fix `backend/windmill-api-workspaces/src/workspaces.rs` points to c580572252, that commit was a subcrate-extraction refactor: the same `WorkspaceSettings` field and `get_settings` query already existed before c580 in `backend/windmill-api/src/workspaces.rs`, with identical logic and no masking. Blame on the pre-BIC file shows the `slack_oauth_client_secret` field and its inclusion in the `get_settings` SELECT came from 700e642c1e ("feat: slack app on workspace level"), while the endpoint itself long predated c580. Therefore c580 is blame drift from moving code, not the commit that introduced the exposure.
evidence: Fix commit 43218c6 adds `if !authed.is_admin { settings.slack_oauth_client_secret = None; }` in `get_settings`, so the bug is exposure of the secret via the general settings endpoint to non-admins.

Blame at pre-fix commit 82e5f6de on `backend/windmill-api-workspaces/src/workspaces.rs` attributes `get_settings` and the `slack_oauth_client_secret` SELECT lines to c580572252, showing why c580 was suspected.

At c580's parent e1a815f6, `backend/windmill-api/src/workspaces.rs` already had `.route("/get_settings", get(get_settings))`, a `WorkspaceSettings` struct containing `pub slack_oauth_client_secret: Option<String>`, and `get_settings` selecting `slack_oauth_client_secret` then directly returning `Ok(Json(settings))` with no admin masking.

Blame on the pre-BIC file e1a815f6 shows `slack_oauth_client_id`/`slack_oauth_client_secret` in `WorkspaceSettings` and the `get_settings` SELECT lines were introduced by 700e642c1e (`feat: slack app on workspace level`), while c580's own message says it merely 'extract[ed] windmill-api-workspaces subcrate', i.e. moved the code.

bug_commits.author: Zoltan Kochan
bug_commits.message: fix: prevent double-loading config error in nested pnpm invocations

llm_verdict.reasoning: The blamed commit modifies environment variable handling for npm invocations, which has nothing to do with package bin directory processing or the path traversal vulnerability. It touches an entirely different file and domain.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: The package manager failed to validate that symlinks within git or file dependencies pointed to locations within the package directory, allowing malicious packages to link to and expose sensitive files outside the package root.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: Creating symlinks in dependencies without validating that the target path is contained within the package directory.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The fix for CVE-2026-24131 patches the `manifest.directories?.bin` branch in `pkg-manager/package-bins/src/index.ts`, adding an `isSubdir(pkgPath, binDir)` check before enumerating files. Blame on the pre-fix file shows those vulnerable lines (`if (manifest.directories?.bin)`, `const binDir = ...`, `const files = await findFiles(binDir)`, and the returned mapped paths) were introduced by commit `f27c3bb0be4b065852dced2b714dafb5459d0eeb` in 2022, not by the suspected commit `8afbb1598445d37985d91fda18abb4795ae5062d`. The suspected BIC only changed `commandsFromBin()` to validate `manifest.bin` entries and added a test for scoped bin-name traversal; the vulnerable `directories.bin` logic already existed unchanged before that commit.
evidence: Fix commit `17432ad5bbed5c2e77255ca6d56a1449bbcfd943` modifies `pkg-manager/package-bins/src/index.ts` at the `manifest.directories?.bin` block, adding `if (!isSubdir(pkgPath, binDir)) return []` before `findFiles(binDir)`.

`blame_lines` on `pkg-manager/package-bins/src/index.ts` at commit `8afbb1598445d37985d91fda18abb4795ae5062d` attributes lines 15-21 of the vulnerable `directories.bin` block to `f27c3bb0be4b065852dced2b714dafb5459d0eeb`, not to `8afbb1598445d37985d91fda18abb4795ae5062d`.

`get_file_at_commit` for prior commit `b8b0c687f2e3403d07381822fe81c08478413916` already contains the same unvalidated `directories.bin` code, proving the vulnerable pattern existed before the suspected BIC.

`get_commit_diff` for `8afbb1598445d37985d91fda18abb4795ae5062d` shows it only rewrote `commandsFromBin()` and added a test for scoped `bin` name traversal; it did not touch the `manifest.directories?.bin` path that the fix later patched.

decomposed_commits.message: fix: prevent double-loading config error in nested pnpm invocations

cves.description: LibreChat MCP Stdio Remote Command Execution

bug_commits.author: Marco Beretta
bug_commits.message: fix: replace unconventional 'void e' with explicit comment to clarify intentionally ignored error

llm_verdict.reasoning: The blamed commit modifies error handling logic in the `getListAgentsHandler` function, which is unrelated to the SSRF vulnerability in the Agent Actions or MCP server remote transports. The vulnerability pertains to missing URL validation logic, whereas the blamed commit only changes a comment and the syntax of an empty catch block.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: The application lacks restrictions on target URLs for Agent Actions and MCP Server remote transports, allowing requests to be made to private IP addresses, localhost, and internal domains.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: Unrestricted URL parsing and fetching where user-provided target endpoints are not validated against a blocklist of private/internal network addresses.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The fix in 24e8a258 adds a second, explicit cleanup step for nested `model_parameters` in `createAgentHandler` and `updateAgentHandler`; the underlying bug is shallow sanitization that left nested empty-string values intact. Blame on the pre-fix file shows the create-side vulnerable line came from `a37bf6719c`, not from 8907bd5d, and the update-side line blamed to 8907bd5d was only a refactor to preserve `avatar: null`. The parent of 8907bd5d already had the same shallow update logic (`const { _id, ...updateData } = removeNullishValues(validatedData);`) with no nested `model_parameters` cleanup, and even earlier revisions passed `req.body` straight through, so the vulnerable behavior clearly predated the suspected BIC.
evidence: Fix commit 24e8a258 patches only `api/server/controllers/agents/v1.js` by adding `removeNullishValues(agentData.model_parameters, true)` / `removeNullishValues(updateData.model_parameters, true)` after existing shallow sanitization.
`blame_lines` on the pre-fix file attributes create-handler lines 109-110 (`const { tools = [], ...agentData } = removeNullishValues(validatedData);`) to commit `a37bf6719c`, not to 8907bd5d.

`get_file_at_commit` for 5b1a31ef4d18cb01f45f791a24e99d15ab7432c4 (the file state before 8907bd5d) already contains `const { _id, ...updateData } = removeNullishValues(validatedData);` in `updateAgentHandler`, with no nested cleanup of `model_parameters`.

`get_file_at_commit` for 3e1591d4042f2031cc2f08dc011e70751d857dc1 shows an even older version where create/update used raw `req.body`, so nested `model_parameters` empty strings were accepted long before 8907bd5d.

The 8907bd5d diff shows its update-handler change was specifically avatar-null preservation (`const { avatar: avatarField, _id, ...rest } = validatedData; const updateData = removeNullishValues(rest); if (avatarField === null) updateData.avatar = avatarField;`), not the introduction of the missing nested sanitization.

cves.description: HomeBox Missing User Authorization

bug_commits.author: Matt Kilgore
bug_commits.message: Update backend/internal/core/services/service_items_attachments.go

llm_verdict.reasoning: The fix commit addresses a Missing Authorization vulnerability by adding Group ID (ctx.GID) checks to AttachmentPath and AttachmentUpdate handlers in the API layer. The blamed commit is a minor logging change in a different file (the internal service layer) and concerns the 'AttachmentAdd' function, which is not the function modified in the fix.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: The provided commit history appears to be unrelated to the actual security vulnerability described (CWE-862: Missing Authorization). The files listed are CI/CD workflow configuration files, suggesting the actual security fix is missing from the provided context or the commit is incorrect.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: N/A - Insufficient information provided in the commit to identify the vulnerable pattern

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The fix addresses missing group-scoped authorization by threading `ctx.GID` into attachment GET/PUT/DELETE paths and adding group filters in the repo layer. In the blamed handler file, the vulnerable GET and PUT calls were not introduced by `606a92b5`; blame on the pre-fix state attributes them to 2022 commits, and the file at `606a92b5`'s parent already contains the same unauthorized calls. `606a92b5` did rewrite parts of the attachment/document implementation, but the underlying authorization gap already existed before that commit in the handler, service, and document/attachment repo code, so this commit carried the bug forward rather than introducing it.
evidence: Blame on `backend/app/api/handlers/v1/v1_ctrl_items_attachments.go` before the fix shows `AttachmentPath(r.Context(), attachmentID)` at line 178 comes from `de419dc37` (2022) and `AttachmentUpdate(ctx, ID, &attachment)` at line 233 comes from `18488f5b1` (2022), not `606a92b5`.

The handler file at `606a92b5`'s parent (`5f63c5f7383dd7fde59a858fac0187fd5962a2ab`) already has the same vulnerable calls: `AttachmentPath(r.Context(), attachmentID)` and `AttachmentUpdate(ctx, ID, &attachment)`.
`606a92b5`'s diff for the handler only adds `primary` form parsing and a commented-out header line; it does not introduce the vulnerable GET/PUT call sites patched by the fix.

Pre-BIC service/repo code already lacked group authorization: `AttachmentPath` called `svc.repo.Attachments.Get(ctx, attachmentID)`, `AttachmentUpdate` used `svc.repo.Attachments.Update(ctx, data.ID, data)` plus `svc.repo.Docs.Rename(ctx, attDoc.ID, data.Title)`, `AttachmentRepo.Get` queried only `attachment.ID(id)`, and `DocumentRepository.Rename/Delete` operated by raw ID without `gid`.

Full generation to update the JS side of things too
In theory the API side works now
Way closer, but has a foreign key constrant issue

Files are actually saved now, but there's still a bug

cves.description: OpenClaw: Unified root-bound write hardening for browser output and related path-boundary flows

bug_commits.author: Albert Lie
bug_commits.message: fix(sandbox): prevent shell option interpretation for paths with leading hyphens

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The fix for GHSA-3pxq-f3cp-jmxp hardens `remove` and `rename` by re-running `assertPathSafety(...)` immediately before the destructive `rm`/`mv` calls, addressing a root-boundary/alias TOCTOU gap in `src/agents/sandbox/fs-bridge.ts`. In the pre-fix blob (`92ded714f3`, visible at commit `18f8393...`), blame shows the security-relevant safety checks came from `c070be1...`/`de61e9c...`, while the underlying `rm`/`mv` flow dates back to `29d78395...`; the suspected commit `c7ae4ed...` only changed the `args: [...]` lines by removing `ensurePathNotInterpretedAsOption(...)`. The same vulnerable structure already existed in the immediately prior file state (`5e3502d...`), so `c7ae4ed...` did not introduce the vulnerability patched by `104d32bb...`.
evidence: Fix commit `104d32bb...` adds extra `assertPathSafety(...)` calls just before `rm` and `mv` in `remove` and `rename`, indicating the vulnerability is insufficiently proximate path-boundary validation for write/unlink flows.

`blame_lines` on the pre-fix file state (`18f8393...`, same blob hash `92ded714f3` as the fix preimage) attributes `remove`/`rename` safety-check lines to `c070be1...` and `de61e9c...`, not to `c7ae4ed...`.

The only lines blamed to `c7ae4ed...` in those functions are `args: [target.containerPath]` / `args: [from.containerPath, to.containerPath]`, matching its diff that merely removes `ensurePathNotInterpretedAsOption(...)`.

`get_file_at_commit` for `5e3502d...` (the file state immediately before `c7ae4ed...`) already shows the same single early `assertPathSafety(...)` checks before `rm`/`mv`, so the vulnerable pattern predated the suspected BIC.

cves.description: OpenClaw ACP client has permission auto-approval bypass via untrusted tool metadata

bug_commits.author: aether-ai-agent
bug_commits.message: fix(security): block dangerous tools from HTTP gateway and fix ACP auto-approval (OC-02)

agent_verdicts.reasoning: `ee31cd47…` did touch the ACP permission path, but the security-relevant logic that the fix actually replaces—`SAFE_AUTO_APPROVE_KINDS`, `resolveToolKindForPermission(...)`, and `promptRequired = !toolName || !isSafeKind || DANGEROUS_ACP_TOOLS.has(toolName)`—was introduced later by `bb1c3dfe…` (and tightened by `153a7644…`). The file state before `ee31…` (`749e28d…`) was already trusting server-provided tool identity and auto-approving non-dangerous tools, so `ee31…` did not introduce the underlying metadata-trust problem; if anything, it was an incomplete hardening/refactor that improved some cases (for example, unknown tool names now prompt). That makes `ee31…` an insufficient mitigation / intermediate refactor, not the commit that introduced the specific vulnerable policy patched by `12cc754…`.
evidence: Pre-fix blame (`b8b43175…`) shows the lines directly replaced by the fix are from `bb1c3dfe107…` and `153a7644…`: `resolveToolKindForPermission(...)` and `promptRequired = !toolName || !isSafeKind || DANGEROUS_ACP_TOOLS.has(toolName)`.
`bb1c3dfe107…` commit diff adds `SAFE_AUTO_APPROVE_KINDS`, the heuristic `resolveToolKindForPermission`, and the `isSafeKind`/`promptRequired` logic that the fix removes.

`749e28de…` (the file state immediately before `ee31…` in `src/acp/client.ts` history) already auto-approved any non-dangerous tool based on server-provided `toolCall.name`, so the trust-in-metadata weakness predates `ee31…`.

`ee31cd47…` rewrote the handler into `resolvePermissionRequest` and added `resolveToolNameForPermission`, but also tightened one case (`promptRequired = !toolName || DANGEROUS_ACP_TOOLS.has(toolName)`), meaning it added/strengthened a security control rather than introducing the later safe-kind auto-approval policy.

decomposed_commits.message: fix(security): block dangerous tools from HTTP gateway and fix ACP auto-approval (OC-02)
